frameworkDecision.reasoning: Tauri v2 wins decisively for this use case. The five non-negotiable requirements — filesystem access, child process spawning, local HTTP server, SQLite, and system tray — are all first-class capabilities in Tauri v2's Rust backend via its plugin ecosystem (tauri-plugin-fs, tauri-plugin-shell, tauri-plugin-sql, tauri-plugin-notification, tauri-plugin-global-shortcut, tauri-plugin-tray). The ReactFlow canvas is pure React/TypeScript running in the WebView, which is identical to a browser environment — no framework-specific integration required. The killer advantage over Electron is bundle size: a Tauri app ships at 2–5 MB vs Electron's 85–120 MB because Tauri uses the OS-native WebView (WKWebView on macOS, WebView2 on Windows, WebKitGTK on Linux) instead of bundling a full Chromium. For a developer-targeting product where bundle size is an explicit concern, this is decisive. Wails v2 (Go backend) is eliminated because: (1) Go's ecosystem for child-process orchestration and OS keychain integration is thinner than Rust's, (2) the SQLite bindings (mattn/go-sqlite3) require CGo which complicates cross-compilation, (3) the Wails community and plugin ecosystem is an order of magnitude smaller than Tauri's, and (4) Go has no meaningful advantage over Rust for this workload profile. Electron is retained as a viable fallback if cross-platform WebView rendering consistency becomes a blocking issue (WKWebView on macOS lacks a few CSS features), but for this product the trade-off strongly favors Tauri.
tradeoffs: Tauri uses OS WebView so CSS/JS rendering can differ slightly across platforms (WKWebView on macOS, WebView2 on Windows) — ReactFlow and modern React are well-tested in both, but edge-case CSS must be verified on each target

Rust learning curve: backend plugin code and custom Tauri commands require Rust; however, 90% of business logic stays in TypeScript (the frontend/WebView), so the Rust surface is limited to system-level glue code
Electron would give a fully predictable Chromium rendering environment and a larger plugin ecosystem (e.g. node-sqlite3, node-keytar) at the cost of an 85+ MB bundle and 200+ MB RAM overhead
Wails v2 is easier to learn (Go) but has no equivalent of Tauri's plugin registry, no native keychain plugin, and weaker Windows support for child process management

Tauri's IPC is message-passing (invoke/emit) rather than direct function calls — all cross-boundary data must be serializable to JSON, which is fine for this use case but rules out passing raw streams directly (solved via channels in Tauri v2)

localDataArchitecture.configLocation: Split model: global config at ~/.agentorg/config.toml (API keys, global preferences, MCP server registrations, update channel), per-project config at {projectRoot}/.agentorg/project.toml (project-level defaults, workspace-relative agent overrides). This mirrors VS Code's user vs workspace settings split. The ~/.agentorg/ directory is created on first launch. The .agentorg/ project directory is committed to git (minus the secrets file) so teams share workflow definitions. A .agentorgignore file (modeled on .gitignore) controls which .agentorg/ files are excluded from git.
localDataArchitecture.workflowFileFormat: YAML with a .workflow.yaml extension stored in {projectRoot}/.agentorg/workflows/. YAML is chosen over JSON because: (1) multiline strings for system prompts are readable without escape sequences, (2) comments are legal (# version history annotations), (3) it diffs cleanly in git. Each workflow file is a standalone, self-describing unit — no database lookup required to understand it. Agent definitions live in {projectRoot}/.agentorg/agents/{agent-name}.agent.yaml. The schema is versioned (apiVersion: agentorg/v1) so future migrations can be handled by a schema migration tool.
localDataArchitecture.workflowYAMLExample:
apiVersion: agentorg/v1
kind: Workflow
metadata:
  name: code-review-pipeline
  description: Reviews a PR diff and posts a summary comment
  tags: [code, review, github]
spec:
  input:
    schema:
      prUrl: string
      focusAreas: string[]
  nodes:
    - id: fetch-diff
      kind: agent
      ref: ./agents/github-fetcher.agent.yaml
      input:
        prUrl: "{{ input.prUrl }}"
    - id: analyze-security
      kind: agent
      ref: ./agents/security-reviewer.agent.yaml
      dependsOn: [fetch-diff]
      input:
        diff: "{{ nodes.fetch-diff.output.diff }}"
        focusAreas: "{{ input.focusAreas }}"
    - id: analyze-logic
      kind: agent
      ref: ./agents/logic-reviewer.agent.yaml
      dependsOn: [fetch-diff]
      input:
        diff: "{{ nodes.fetch-diff.output.diff }}"
    - id: human-gate
      kind: human_gate
      dependsOn: [analyze-security, analyze-logic]
      timeout: 3600
      prompt: "Review the analysis below and approve to post comment."
    - id: post-comment
      kind: agent
      ref: ./agents/github-commenter.agent.yaml
      dependsOn: [human-gate]
      condition: "{{ nodes.human-gate.decision == 'approve' }}"
  output:
    commentUrl: "{{ nodes.post-comment.output.url }}"
localDataArchitecture.apiKeyStorage: Dual-layer strategy: (1) Primary — OS keychain via the tauri-plugin-keychain crate, which calls Security.framework (macOS Keychain), Windows Credential Manager, or libsecret (Linux GNOME Keyring) depending on platform. Each API key is stored as a separate keychain entry with service=agentorg and account={providerId}:{keyId}. This provides hardware-backed encryption on macOS (Secure Enclave on M-series) and integrates with the OS lock screen. (2) Fallback for headless/CI environments — AES-256-GCM encrypted file at ~/.agentorg/secrets.enc, with the encryption key derived from a machine-specific secret (macOS: IOPlatformUUID, Windows: MachineGuid, Linux: /etc/machine-id) XOR'd with a user-set master passphrase. The master passphrase is never stored — it is prompted at launch and kept in process memory only. The keychain approach is the default; file-based is opt-in via config.
localDataArchitecture.runHistoryStorage: SQLite database at ~/.agentorg/history.db (global, cross-project) using the tauri-plugin-sql plugin with the sqlcipher feature enabled for encryption at rest. Schema: runs(id, workflow_id, workflow_path, project_root, status, input_json, output_json, started_at, completed_at, error), run_events(id, run_id, seq, ts, level, node_id, agent_id, event_type, payload_json), run_costs(id, run_id, node_id, model_id, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_usd). The database is opened with PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL for concurrent read performance. Run events are pruned after 90 days by a background job triggered on app launch. A secondary per-project SQLite at {projectRoot}/.agentorg/runs.db stores only run metadata (no full event logs) for git-committed project history — this allows teammates to see historical run summaries without the full event payload.
localDataArchitecture.workspaceModel: Workspace = a directory the user has opened, exactly like VS Code. The app maintains a recent workspaces list in ~/.agentorg/config.toml. Opening a workspace loads all .workflow.yaml and .agent.yaml files from {workspaceRoot}/.agentorg/ into the canvas. Multiple workspaces can be open in separate windows (Tauri supports multi-window via WebviewWindowBuilder). A workspace can optionally define an .agentorg/workspace.toml that declares shared variables (e.g. default model, shared tool configs) inherited by all workflows in that workspace. There is no concept of a 'project' separate from a workspace — the filesystem directory is the unit of organization, enabling trivial git integration.

executionEngine.agentProcessModel: Each agent node executes as an async Rust task (tokio::spawn) within the Tauri backend process, NOT as a separate OS process. This is the critical design decision: spawning a new OS process per agent adds 50-200ms overhead per node invocation, which compounds badly in parallel fan-out workflows. Instead, the Tauri backend maintains a tokio runtime with a configurable worker thread pool (default: num_cpus * 2). Each agent node is a Future that calls the LLM API over HTTP, processes the streaming response, and emits events via Tauri's event channel. For tool executions that involve running user commands (bash, python scripts), THOSE spawn OS child processes via tauri-plugin-shell with explicit allowlists. This hybrid model gives sub-millisecond task dispatch for pure LLM calls while maintaining process isolation for shell tool execution. An 'agent' in the execution model is a Rust struct AgentRunner { node_id, agent_def, input, run_id } implementing a run() async method. The workflow executor holds a JoinSet<AgentResult> and fans out parallel branches by spawning multiple tokio tasks simultaneously.
executionEngine.filesystemAccess: Configurable scope model with three tiers. Tier 1 (Sandboxed, default for untrusted workflows): agents can only read/write within the current workspace directory and ~/.agentorg/tmp/. Enforced by the tauri-plugin-fs scope configuration in tauri.conf.json — all file operations go through Tauri's scoped FS plugin which validates paths before syscalls. Tier 2 (Project-scoped): the workflow YAML can declare an expanded scope with explicit path allowlists — user approves on first run via a native permission dialog. Tier 3 (Full access): power users can grant unrestricted FS access per-workflow via the UI, which stores a capability grant in the project config. Tauri v2's capability system handles all three tiers through its permissions manifest without custom path validation code in Rust.

builtInTools.name: read_file
builtInTools.description: Reads file content as UTF-8 string or base64 for binary files. Respects the workflow's FS scope tier. Supports glob patterns for reading multiple files. Returns {content, mimeType, sizeBytes, lastModified}.

builtInTools.name: write_file
builtInTools.description: Writes or appends content to a file within the allowed scope. Creates parent directories if they don't exist (configurable). Returns {path, bytesWritten}.

builtInTools.name: list_directory
builtInTools.description: Lists directory contents with optional recursive flag and glob filter. Returns {entries: [{name, type, sizeBytes, lastModified}]}. Respects FS scope.

builtInTools.name: run_command
builtInTools.description: Spawns a shell command via tauri-plugin-shell with an explicit per-workflow command allowlist (declared in workflow YAML under spec.tools.allowedCommands). Streams stdout/stderr back as events. Returns {exitCode, stdout, stderr, durationMs}. Never executes unlisted commands.

builtInTools.name: http_request
builtInTools.description: Makes outbound HTTP/HTTPS requests via Tauri's HTTP plugin. Supports GET/POST/PUT/DELETE, custom headers, JSON body, streaming response. Domain allowlist configurable per workflow.

builtInTools.name: search_web
builtInTools.description: Performs a web search using the configured search provider (Brave Search API or SearXNG self-hosted). Returns {results: [{title, url, snippet}]}. API key stored in keychain under service=agentorg, account=search-provider.

builtInTools.name: git_status
builtInTools.description: Runs git status, git log, git diff in the workspace directory via run_command. Returns structured JSON parsed from git output. Pre-approved in the default command allowlist for git-aware workflows.

builtInTools.name: git_commit
builtInTools.description: Stages specified files and creates a git commit with a provided message. Requires human_gate node approval before execution when used in automated workflows (enforced by the workflow engine).

builtInTools.name: read_clipboard
builtInTools.description: Reads current clipboard text content via Tauri's clipboard plugin. Useful for 'process what I just copied' workflow triggers.

builtInTools.name: notify
builtInTools.description: Sends a native desktop notification with title, body, and optional action buttons. Used by agents to request human attention mid-run without blocking workflow execution.

builtInTools.name: mcp_call
builtInTools.description: Connects to a registered local MCP server (listed in ~/.agentorg/config.toml under [mcp_servers]) via stdio or HTTP transport and invokes a tool by name. Returns the MCP tool result as JSON. MCP server processes are managed by the Tauri backend — started on demand, kept alive for the session, restarted on crash.

builtInTools.name: invoke_agent
builtInTools.description: Calls another agent node within the same workflow by nodeId, passing explicit input. Used by orchestrator agents to delegate sub-tasks. This is the dynamic dispatch mechanism — the workflow YAML can declare static edges, but orchestrator agents can also invoke nodes dynamically via this tool.

executionEngine.mcpIntegration: MCP servers are registered in ~/.agentorg/config.toml as [[mcp_servers]] entries with fields: name, transport ('stdio' or 'http'), command (for stdio: e.g. 'npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem'), args, env, url (for HTTP transport). The Tauri backend maintains a McpServerPool: a HashMap<server_name, McpClient> of persistent connections. On app launch, servers marked autostart=true are started immediately; others start on first use. The mcp_call built-in tool resolves the server by name, ensures the connection is live (reconnects if dropped), and calls the MCP tool via the MCP protocol. Stdio servers are managed as child processes via tokio::process::Command — the Tauri backend owns their lifecycle and can restart them. Tool schemas from all registered MCP servers are fetched at startup (tools/list) and made available to agents, so they appear in the agent tool picker UI alongside built-in tools.
executionEngine.streamingToUI: Tauri v2 Channels (tauri::ipc::Channel) are used for streaming, not Tauri events. Channels are the correct primitive for high-throughput ordered streams — they are typed, backpressure-aware, and avoid the string serialization overhead of window.emit(). The workflow executor holds a Channel<RunEvent> per run. Each tokio task (agent node) receives a clone of the channel's sender half. As LLM tokens arrive from the HTTP streaming response (via reqwest with stream feature), they are parsed and emitted as RunEvent::TokenChunk { node_id, seq, content } through the channel. The frontend receives these via the channel's onmessage callback and routes them to the correct ReactFlow node by node_id. The channel is closed when the run terminates. Backpressure: if the frontend falls behind (e.g. React render is slow), the channel's internal buffer fills and the Rust sender awaits — this naturally throttles LLM token processing without dropping events. For the VS Code extension connection (which connects over HTTP, not IPC), the Tauri backend mirrors all RunEvents to the local HTTP SSE endpoint simultaneously.

ipcArchitecture.approach: Tauri v2 uses a three-primitive IPC model: (1) Commands (tauri::command) for request-response operations — the frontend calls invoke('command_name', {args}) and receives a Promise<Result>. Used for: loading workflows, saving files, starting/cancelling runs, fetching run history from SQLite. (2) Channels (tauri::ipc::Channel) for ordered high-throughput streams — used for streaming run events (token chunks, node status changes, cost updates) from the Rust backend to the React frontend in real time. A channel is created per run start and its ID is returned in the invoke('start_run') response; the frontend subscribes to it immediately. (3) Tauri Events (window.emit / listen) for broadcast notifications — used for system-level signals like active run count changes (for tray badge update), app update availability, and MCP server health changes. Commands and channels are the primary IPC surfaces; events handle loose coupling for UI elements not directly involved in a run.
ipcArchitecture.vscodeConnectionMethod: The Tauri backend spawns a local HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:{dynamic_port} using axum (a Rust async HTTP framework that runs inside the tokio runtime — no separate process required). The port is randomly assigned at startup (avoiding conflicts) and written to ~/.agentorg/ipc.json: {port, authToken, pid, startedAt}. The VS Code extension reads this file to discover the port and auth token. All VS Code extension-to-desktop-app communication uses standard HTTP: REST endpoints for triggering workflows and querying run status, SSE for subscribing to run event streams. Authentication uses a Bearer token (the authToken from ipc.json) — it is a randomly generated 256-bit hex string, valid for the lifetime of the app process. The VS Code extension also watches ipc.json for changes (via vscode.workspace.createFileSystemWatcher) to detect app restarts and reconnect. This HTTP approach means the VS Code extension works identically whether the desktop app is Tauri, Electron, or any future replacement — the protocol is decoupled from the framework. WebSockets are available as a fallback if SSE reconnection proves unreliable on Windows, but SSE with Last-Event-ID resumption is the default.

desktopUXFeatures.feature: Global hotkey to trigger a workflow from anywhere
desktopUXFeatures.implementation: tauri-plugin-global-shortcut registers a configurable hotkey (default: Cmd+Shift+A on macOS, Ctrl+Shift+A on Windows/Linux) at the OS level. When triggered, the Tauri backend emits an event to the frontend which opens a Command Palette overlay (floating window via WebviewWindowBuilder with decorations=false, always_on_top=true, transparent background). The palette shows recent workflows and a fuzzy search box. The user types a workflow name or trigger phrase, selects it, and the run starts immediately. If the app window is hidden (in tray), the palette appears without surfacing the main window. A secondary hotkey (Cmd+Shift+R) triggers 'Run on current selection' — it reads clipboard content (or asks the active app via Accessibility APIs on macOS) and runs the last-used workflow with that content as input.

desktopUXFeatures.feature: System tray for monitoring active runs
desktopUXFeatures.implementation: tauri-plugin-tray creates a persistent tray icon. The icon has three states: idle (static icon), active (animated spinner using a sequence of PNG frames cycled via a 100ms Rust interval), and attention-needed (pulsing icon when a human_gate node is awaiting approval). The tray menu (built with MenuBuilder) shows: current active run count, a list of up to 5 recent runs with status badges, a 'New Run' submenu listing the 5 most recently used workflows, and 'Show Window' / 'Quit' actions. The tray badge on macOS shows the count of awaiting-human-gate runs. Run status changes update the tray menu in real time by calling tray.set_menu() from the Rust event handler. On Windows, a balloon notification fires when a long run completes while the window is hidden.

tauri-plugin-dialog exposes open() and save() which call the OS-native file picker (NSOpenPanel on macOS, IFileOpenDialog on Windows, GTK FileChooserDialog on Linux). In the ReactFlow canvas, workflow input nodes of type 'file' render a 'Browse' button that calls invoke('pick_file', {filters, multiple}) — no Electron-style dialog.showOpenDialog workaround needed. For agent tool definitions, file path parameters show a path input with a folder icon that triggers the native picker. The returned path is validated against the workflow's FS scope before being passed to the agent. File paths are stored as workspace-relative paths in workflow YAML (e.g. ./data/input.csv) so workflows remain portable.

Tauri v2's drag-and-drop support is handled at two levels. Level 1 — dropping onto the canvas background: the WebView intercepts the dragover and drop DOM events; dropped file paths are extracted from the DataTransfer object (Tauri populates event.dataTransfer.files with native File objects including the real filesystem path). The frontend creates a new Input Node at the drop coordinates pre-populated with the file path. Level 2 — dropping onto an existing agent node: the node highlight style activates on dragenter, and on drop the file path is injected into the agent node's input mapping for the 'file' parameter. For multiple files dropped simultaneously, a 'Batch Input' node is created that fans out to the target agent. Tauri's onDragDrop window event is also registered as a fallback for drops that land outside the WebView area.

tauri-plugin-notification sends native OS notifications (UserNotifications on macOS, Windows Toast Notifications, libnotify on Linux). Notifications fire on: (1) run completion — title: workflow name, body: 'Completed in 4.2s — 3 nodes, $0.012', action button: 'View Results' which focuses the app window and opens the run detail panel; (2) run failure — body includes the failing node name and truncated error message; (3) human gate waiting — body: 'Approval needed: [node name]', action button: 'Review' which opens the approval dialog directly. Notification preferences are per-workflow (configurable in the workflow settings panel). On macOS, notifications use the app's bundle identifier for grouping and support notification categories with action buttons registered via UNNotificationCategory. Silent notifications (no sound) are used for routine completions; sound notifications for failures and human gates.

desktopUXFeatures.feature: Run on current file context menu integration
desktopUXFeatures.implementation: macOS: A Finder Sync Extension (bundled as a separate target in the Tauri app's Xcode build phase) registers a context menu item 'Run AgentOrg Workflow...' for files matching configured extensions (*.ts, *.py, *.md, etc. — configurable). The extension communicates with the main app via XPC. When triggered, it sends the file path to the main app which opens a workflow picker sheet showing workflows that accept file input. Windows: A registry entry under HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\*\shell\AgentOrg is written by the installer (NSIS/WiX, generated by Tauri's bundler) to add the context menu item. The registered command launches the app with a --context-file flag if not running, or signals the running instance via a named pipe. Linux: A .desktop file action is registered in ~/.local/share/applications/. The workflow picker is a lightweight floating window (400x500px, no titlebar) that appears near the cursor position.

criticalConsiderations: WebView2 on Windows requires a separate bootstrapper or must be bundled — Tauri's NSIS/WiX installer handles WebView2 bootstrapping automatically, but the app will not launch on Windows machines without WebView2 Runtime (pre-installed on Windows 11, requires install on Windows 10). Test the installer flow on a clean Windows 10 VM before shipping.

WKWebView on macOS 13+ disables SharedArrayBuffer unless the page serves Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy and Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy headers — ReactFlow itself does not require SharedArrayBuffer, but if any canvas worker (e.g. layout computation with elkjs) uses it, the Tauri custom protocol handler must inject these headers.

SQLite with sqlcipher requires the SQLCIPHER_PASSPHRASE environment variable to be set before opening the database — in Tauri this is done in the Rust setup() hook before the plugin initializes. The passphrase must be derived from a stable machine secret (not hardcoded) to survive app restarts without user prompt.

Tauri v2's capability system (permissions manifest in src-tauri/capabilities/) replaces v1's allowlist — every plugin API the frontend can call must be explicitly declared. Forgetting a capability causes silent 'not allowed' errors at runtime. Define capabilities incrementally and test each plugin surface during development.

The local HTTP server for VS Code extension connectivity must bind only to 127.0.0.1 (loopback), never 0.0.0.0. The auth token in ipc.json must have file permissions 0600 (owner read/write only). On macOS, the app's network entitlement (com.apple.security.network.server) must be declared in the entitlements plist or the HTTP server binding will be silently blocked by the app sandbox.

ReactFlow with 100+ nodes has known performance issues with the default SVG edge renderer — switch to the useEdgesUpdater pattern and enable ReactFlow's nodesDraggable=false during active runs to prevent accidental graph mutations while a workflow is executing. Use React.memo on custom node components to prevent re-renders on every streaming token event.

LLM API calls from the Rust backend must use reqwest with a custom client that sets timeout(Duration::from_secs(120)) and tcp_keepalive(Duration::from_secs(30)) — without keepalive, long streaming responses on macOS are dropped after 60s by the OS TCP stack on idle connections.

Code-signing is mandatory for macOS distribution outside the App Store — unsigned apps are quarantined by Gatekeeper and require user bypass. Tauri's CI workflow supports codesign and notarization via the APPLE_CERTIFICATE and APPLE_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD environment variables. Budget time for Apple Developer Program enrollment and notarization turnaround.

The workflow YAML format must be treated as a public API from day one — breaking changes to the schema will invalidate users' git-committed workflow files. Version the schema with apiVersion: agentorg/v1, write a migration CLI (agentorg migrate) before making any breaking changes, and publish a JSON Schema for IDE validation in VS Code via the yaml-language-server comment directive.

coreCapabilities.capability: Inline workflow triggering via right-click context menu
coreCapabilities.why: Developers are already in their editor looking at a file. The zero-friction path is right-click → 'Run workflow on this file'. Without this, they must context-switch to the desktop app or CLI. This single feature justifies the install.
coreCapabilities.implementation: Register a contributes.menus entry for editor/context and explorer/context. The command 'agentorg.runWorkflowOnFile' reads the active file URI, queries .agentorg/ for workflows that declare file-type compatibility in their input schema (e.g. accepts: ['*.ts', '*.py']), and presents a QuickPick filtered list. Selected workflow is dispatched to the in-process engine (Model C hybrid). File path is injected as the workflow input payload. This also registers a keybinding (default: Ctrl+Shift+W / Cmd+Shift+W) for the same action on the active editor.

coreCapabilities.why: Passive awareness of background agent runs is critical. Developers trigger a workflow and return to coding. Without a status bar item they have no idea if the run is still going, succeeded, or needs their approval at a human gate.
coreCapabilities.implementation: A StatusBarController class (singleton, activated on extension load) registers a vscode.StatusBarItem with alignment=Right, priority=100. It subscribes to the RunEventBus (internal EventEmitter). States: idle (hidden), running ($(loading~spin) 2 agents active), awaiting-human ($(bell) Approval needed — amber color, clicking opens the HumanGatePanel), completed ($(check) Done in 3.2s — clears after 5s), failed ($(error) Run failed — red, persistent until dismissed). The status bar text is clickable and opens the Active Runs sidebar panel. Multiple concurrent runs show the count: $(loading~spin) 3 runs active.

coreCapabilities.capability: Sidebar: Agent Library, Workflow List, Active Run Monitor
coreCapabilities.why: The sidebar is the persistent home for the extension. Developers need to browse what agents exist, what workflows are configured, and watch live runs without opening a separate app.
coreCapabilities.implementation: Register a contributes.views container 'agentorg' with icon from .agentorg/icons. Three TreeView providers: (1) WorkflowTreeProvider — reads .agentorg/workflows/*.yaml, groups by tag, shows last-run status icon, supports drag-to-run. (2) AgentLibraryTreeProvider — reads .agentorg/agents/*.yaml, shows model badge, context menu to edit/duplicate. (3) ActiveRunsTreeProvider — live-updated from RunEventBus, shows per-run status and nested node-level status. Each tree node has inline action buttons (codicon icons) via TreeItem.command and contributes.menus/view/item/context. The Run tree supports expanding a run to see per-node status, streaming output preview (truncated to 3 lines), cost so far, and an [Approve] / [Reject] button for human-gate nodes.

coreCapabilities.why: VS Code developers live in the command palette. Discoverability and keyboard-driven workflows are table stakes for a 'must install' extension.
coreCapabilities.implementation: Register at minimum: 'agentorg.runWorkflow' (fuzzy search across all workflows), 'agentorg.createAgent' (opens a multi-step input wizard using vscode.window.showInputBox/showQuickPick sequence), 'agentorg.openWorkflowInDesktop' (IPC to desktop app — graceful degradation if not running), 'agentorg.showRunHistory' (opens Run History panel), 'agentorg.cancelRun' (lists active runs to cancel), 'agentorg.approveHumanGate', 'agentorg.configureSettings'. All commands are in the 'Agent Organizer' category for grouping.

coreCapabilities.capability: Chat panel for multi-agent workflow invocation
coreCapabilities.why: Natural language is the lowest-friction trigger. A developer can type 'refactor this file to use async/await and add tests' and the extension maps that intent to the right workflow, similar to GitHub Copilot Chat but executing multi-agent pipelines instead of single-model completions.
coreCapabilities.implementation: Implemented as a WebviewPanel (not using the vscode.chat API which is GitHub Copilot-specific) with a custom React/Preact UI bundled separately. The panel has a chat input, a workflow selector chip (pre-selects best-match workflow from NLP intent detection), and a streaming output display. Messages are sent to the in-process engine. Streaming tokens appear in a virtual-scroll list. The panel is opened via 'agentorg.openChat' command or the sidebar header action. It persists state across panel visibility toggles using getState/setState on the webview. Context awareness: the panel automatically includes the active file, selected text, and workspace root in the workflow input metadata.

coreCapabilities.capability: Inline diff view for agent-proposed code changes
coreCapabilities.why: When an agent modifies code, the developer must review and accept/reject before the file is written. This is the core trust boundary — without it the extension is unsafe for production codebases.
coreCapabilities.implementation: When a workflow produces file mutation events (output type 'file_patch' containing a unified diff), the extension stages the proposed changes in a temporary in-memory document and calls vscode.commands.executeCommand('vscode.diff', originalUri, proposedUri, 'Agent Proposal: filename.ts'). The diff uses VS Code's native diff editor — familiar to all developers. A custom CodeLens provider injects [Accept All] [Reject All] [Accept Hunk] buttons above each changed section. Accepting writes via vscode.workspace.applyEdit using WorkspaceEdit. Rejecting clears the proposed document. For multi-file changes, a ProposedChangesPanel (TreeView) lists all pending file modifications before any are applied.

coreCapabilities.capability: Workflow YAML/JSON intellisense via Language Server
coreCapabilities.why: Developers editing .agentorg/*.yaml files need autocomplete, hover docs, and error detection. Without this, editing workflow configs is error-prone and requires reading external docs.
coreCapabilities.implementation: A dedicated language server process (see languageServerDesign) provides completions, hover, diagnostics, and go-to-definition for all .agentorg/ config files. This is the feature that makes the extension indispensable for the workflow author role — equivalent to how the Python extension makes editing Python files authoritative.

connectionModel.choice: Model C: Hybrid — standalone for all execution, desktop app integration as optional enhancement
connectionModel.reasoning: Model A (desktop-required) is a distribution liability: the extension would be uninstallable without a separate native app running, which breaks the VS Code extension mental model and would generate 1-star reviews from developers who just want to try it. Claude Code's standalone model proves users expect zero external dependencies. Model B (fully standalone) is technically correct but misses integration value — developers who have the desktop app open on their second monitor should be able to jump to the visual canvas with one click. Model C gives the best of both: the extension ships a bundled TypeScript workflow engine (same engine package as the desktop app and CLI — this is the key architectural constraint) that runs in the VS Code extension host process. This means zero external dependency for workflow execution. When the desktop app IS running, the extension detects it on a well-known local port (default: 57210) via an HTTP health check at activation time. If detected, enhanced features are unlocked: 'Open in Designer' command launches the canvas to that workflow, live run status syncs bidirectionally, and agent configs edited in the desktop app hot-reload into the extension. The hybrid detection uses a 2-second timeout health check — if the desktop app is not on that port, the extension silently continues in standalone mode with no user-visible degradation. This mirrors how the Live Share extension works — full standalone value, richer experience when the server is present.
connectionModel.architectureDetails: Shared engine package: packages/workflow-engine (TypeScript) is published as an npm package consumed by the desktop app (Electron), CLI (Node.js), and this extension (VS Code extension host). The engine is pure TypeScript with no native bindings — critical for running inside the VS Code extension host which has Node.js but not arbitrary native modules. The engine exposes: WorkflowRunner class, AgentRegistry, RunEventBus (EventEmitter). API key access: standalone mode reads from .agentorg/secrets.enc (AES-256-GCM encrypted file, key derived from OS keychain via the 'keytar' module which VS Code ships natively). Desktop IPC: when the desktop app is detected, the extension registers as a secondary client on the desktop app's local IPC socket (Unix domain socket on macOS/Linux, named pipe on Windows) using the existing protocol defined in the backend architecture. Run events flow over this socket. The extension never proxies API calls through the desktop app — LLM calls always go directly from the extension host to the provider APIs, avoiding latency and avoiding the desktop app being a single point of failure.

languageServerDesign.implementation: The language server runs as a separate Node.js process spawned by the extension's activate() function using vscode-languageclient/node. This is the standard pattern (same as the Python extension, YAML extension, JSON Schema extension). It must be a separate process — not in-process — because YAML parsing with schema validation is CPU-intensive and would block the extension host's event loop, causing VS Code UI jank. The server binary is bundled in the extension at dist/server/agentorgLanguageServer.js. It uses the 'yaml-language-server' as a base (it already handles YAML parsing and JSON Schema validation) extended with a custom AgentOrgSchemaProvider that generates live JSON Schema from the actual agents and workflows in the workspace's .agentorg/ folder. Schema is regenerated on file-watcher events (chokidar watches .agentorg/**/*.yaml). The server communicates with the extension client over stdio. The client is initialized in the extension's activate() with a DocumentSelector targeting { scheme: 'file', pattern: '**/.agentorg/**/*.{yaml,json,yml}' }.
capabilities: Completion — agent name completions in workflow YAML: when the cursor is at 'agentId:' the server reads the AgentRegistry (loaded from .agentorg/agents/) and returns a CompletionList with each agent's id, name (as detail), and first line of system prompt (as documentation). Model ID completions: when cursor is at 'modelId:' returns all registered model IDs with provider prefix and context window in the detail field. Step reference completions: in 'inputMapping:' expressions, completes nodeId references from the workflow's own nodes array. Trigger characters: ':', '$', '{' (for JSONata expressions in inputMapping/outputMapping).

Hover — hovering over an agentId value shows a HoverCard with the agent's full config: model, system prompt preview (first 200 chars), tools list, temperature, token limits. Hovering over a modelId shows provider, context window, cost per 1k tokens. Hovering over a workflow node reference shows the node's type and label. Rendered as Markdown in the hover popup.

Diagnostics — real-time validation: missing required fields (severity: Error), unknown agentId references that do not match any .agentorg/agents/*.yaml file (Error), cyclic workflow graphs detected via DFS (Error, with a path trace in the message), unreachable nodes with no incoming edges (Warning), human_gate nodes with no timeout configured (Warning, links to docs), deprecated model IDs (Information). Diagnostics refresh on save and on 250ms debounced keystroke.

Go-to-definition — pressing F12 on an agentId value opens the corresponding .agentorg/agents/<agentId>.yaml file. On a modelId, opens the provider's models section in .agentorg/providers.yaml. On a nodeId reference in an edge definition, jumps to that node's definition within the same workflow file using a DocumentLink.

Code actions — quick-fix actions for diagnostics: 'Create missing agent' (scaffolds a new agent YAML with the referenced id), 'Remove unreachable node', 'Add default humanGateTimeout'. Invoked via the lightbulb menu (Ctrl+. / Cmd+.).

Rename symbols — renaming an agentId in its definition file triggers a WorkspaceEdit that updates all workflow YAML files that reference that agentId. Uses the server's cross-file reference index built from the .agentorg/ watcher.

Folding ranges — collapses workflow node definitions, edge lists, and systemPrompt multiline strings. The systemPrompt field often contains hundreds of lines; folding it keeps workflow files navigable.

Semantic tokens — highlights agentId values with a distinct token color (separate from string literals), nodeId references in a different color, JSONata expressions in inputMapping/outputMapping with expression-aware highlighting (identifiers vs operators vs string literals within the expression).

runVisualization.activeAgentIndicator: Three simultaneous channels for indicating which agent is active: (1) Sidebar — the Active Runs tree node for the currently-executing agent node gets a spinning $(loading~spin) codicon prefixed to its label, replacing the idle $(circle-outline). The tree auto-reveals (tree.reveal()) the active node so it is always visible without manual scrolling. (2) Editor decorations — a vscode.TextEditorDecorationType with a left-margin gutter icon (a pulsing dot rendered as an SVG data URI, animated via CSS) is applied to the file currently being processed by the active agent. This uses the same mechanism as the Git blame gutter — visible without cluttering the code. The decoration is cleared when the agent completes. (3) Status bar — the status bar item shows the agent name truncated to 20 chars: $(loading~spin) ReviewAgent analyzing... This is the minimal-noise signal for developers who have minimized the sidebar.
runVisualization.outputDisplay: Streaming output uses a custom OutputChannel named 'Agent Organizer: [run label]' created per run via vscode.window.createOutputChannel(). This is the correct VS Code primitive for streaming text — it appends lines without requiring a webview and supports ANSI color codes (OutputChannel.appendLine with vscode.window.createOutputChannel(name, {log: false})). The channel is shown automatically when a run starts (channel.show(true) — the second arg preserves focus on the editor). For the active-agent streaming tokens (high-frequency, 30-50 tokens/second), a separate channel named 'Agent Organizer: Live Stream' is used. Tokens are batched into 100ms flush intervals (setInterval flushing a local buffer) to prevent the channel from becoming a performance bottleneck. For the chat panel, streaming renders inside the WebviewPanel's React component using the same double-buffer technique from the desktop frontend (accumulate in rawBuffer, flush on requestAnimationFrame). The chat panel is the primary UX surface; the output channel is the secondary log surface for non-interactive runs (e.g. right-click triggered workflows).
runVisualization.diffFlow: When a workflow node emits output of type 'file_patch': (1) The RunEventBus fires a 'filePatchProposed' event with payload { runId, nodeId, patches: FilePatch[] } where FilePatch = { uri: string, unifiedDiff: string }. (2) The DiffManager (extension-side class) intercepts this event and creates an in-memory TextDocument for each patched file by applying the unified diff to the current file content using the 'diff' npm package (already in the shared engine). The proposed content is stored in a Map<uri, ProposedDocument>. (3) vscode.commands.executeCommand('vscode.diff', vscode.Uri.file(originalPath), proposedInMemoryUri, 'Agent Proposal: ' + path.basename(originalPath), { preview: true }) is called. The preview flag prevents the diff tab from becoming a permanent editor tab. (4) A CodeLens provider (registered for the scheme 'agentorg-proposal') injects [Accept] [Reject] [Open in Designer] lenses at the top of each changed block (identified by parsing the unified diff hunk headers). (5) Accept calls workspace.applyEdit with a WorkspaceEdit that replaces file content. Reject removes the entry from ProposedDocument map and closes the diff tab. (6) If the run produces changes to multiple files, a ProposalSummaryPanel (TreeView in the sidebar) lists all pending proposals with file names, line change counts, and per-file accept/reject buttons. A global [Accept All] button at the top. No changes are written to disk until explicitly accepted.
runVisualization.humanGateUI: Human-gate approval is surfaced in three places depending on developer context: (1) If the sidebar is open: the Active Runs tree node for the blocked node shows $(bell) AWAITING APPROVAL in amber color with two inline action buttons [Approve] [Reject] in the tree item's buttons array (contributes.menus/view/item/context). (2) Status bar becomes amber and shows $(bell) Approval needed — clicking it calls 'agentorg.approveHumanGate' which first presents a QuickPick showing the gate's prompt text and [Approve] [Reject] options. This covers the common case where the developer is focused in an editor with sidebar closed. (3) If the workflow was triggered from the chat panel, a dedicated approval card appears inline in the chat thread — same visual treatment as a chat message but with large Approve/Reject buttons and the agent's last message shown as context. The approval card is rendered inside the WebviewPanel and is keyboard accessible (Tab to focus, Enter to approve, Escape to reject). All three surfaces dispatch to the same runStore.resolveHumanGate() action which POSTs to the engine's signal endpoint. The human gate card shows a countdown timer if the workflow has humanGateTimeout configured — a CSS progress bar drains over the timeout duration. When the timer reaches zero, the gate auto-resolves per the node's on_timeout policy (approve_by_default or reject_by_default) and a warning notification fires.

commands: agentorg.runWorkflow — run a named workflow with optional input payload; returns a RunHandle object with .on() for events and .cancel()
agentorg.runWorkflowOnFile — run a workflow on a specific file URI; auto-selects compatible workflows
agentorg.createAgent — open the agent creation wizard; returns AgentConfig on completion
agentorg.openWorkflowInDesktop — open a workflow by ID in the desktop designer (no-op if desktop app not running)
agentorg.showRunHistory — open the run history panel for a workflow ID
agentorg.cancelRun — cancel a running workflow by run ID
agentorg.approveHumanGate — approve or reject a pending human gate with optional note
agentorg.getActiveRuns — returns array of ActiveRun objects for programmatic consumers
agentorg.refreshWorkflows — force-reload workflow definitions from disk (useful after external edits)
agentorg.exportRunTrace — export a run's full execution trace as JSON for debugging
events: agentorg.onRunStarted — fires with { runId, workflowId, workflowName, input } when any run starts
agentorg.onRunCompleted — fires with { runId, workflowId, output, durationMs, costUsd } on success
agentorg.onRunFailed — fires with { runId, workflowId, error, nodeId } on failure
agentorg.onHumanGatePending — fires with { runId, nodeId, promptText, timeoutMs } when a gate opens
agentorg.onFilePatchProposed — fires with { runId, nodeId, patches: FilePatch[] } before any diff is applied; consumers can intercept and cancel
agentorg.onAgentTokenStream — fires with { runId, nodeId, agentId, token } on each streamed token; high-frequency, use sparingly
agentorg.onWorkflowsChanged — fires when .agentorg/ files change on disk
settings: agentorg.workflowsPath — path to the .agentorg folder relative to workspace root (default: .agentorg)
agentorg.desktopAppPort — local port for desktop app IPC detection (default: 57210)
agentorg.autoShowOutputOnRun — whether to auto-focus the output channel when a run starts (default: true)
agentorg.maxConcurrentRuns — max simultaneous workflow runs in the extension host (default: 3)
agentorg.humanGateNotificationSound — play a sound when a human gate opens (default: true)
agentorg.diffAutoOpen — automatically open diff view when agent proposes file changes (default: true)
agentorg.streamingBatchIntervalMs — how often to flush streaming tokens to output channel (default: 100)
agentorg.telemetry — opt-in usage telemetry for improving the extension (default: false)
agentorg.logLevel — extension host log verbosity: error | warn | info | debug (default: warn)

vscode.securityModel: "The extension operates under a three-tier permission model. Tier 1 — File access: The extension ONLY reads and writes files within the open workspace. vscode.workspace.workspaceFolders is the boundary. All file reads go through vscode.workspace.fs (the VS Code virtual filesystem API) rather than Node.js fs directly — this ensures compatibility with remote workspaces (SSH, WSL, Codespaces) and respects VS Code's file permission model. Agent-proposed file writes are NEVER applied automatically — they always go through the diff-and-accept flow described in runVisualization. The extension refuses to operate outside the workspace boundary at the engine level: WorkflowRunner is initialized with a sandboxedRoot parameter and all file tool paths are validated against it before execution. Tier 2 — Terminal command execution: Agents with shell tool access create a vscode.Terminal via vscode.window.createTerminal({ name: 'Agent: ' + agentName, hideFromUser: false }) and send commands via terminal.sendText(). The terminal is ALWAYS visible to the user — no hidden process spawning. For sandboxing, the extension checks if the workspace has a .agentorg/permissions.yaml file that explicitly lists allowed command patterns (glob-style: e.g. 'npm test', 'pytest *', 'tsc --noEmit'). Commands not matching the allowlist are blocked and the developer is prompted to approve with a modal (vscode.window.showWarningMessage with modal: true and [Allow Once] [Allow Always] [Deny] buttons). 'Allow Always' appends the pattern to .agentorg/permissions.yaml. Tier 3 — API key management: API keys are NEVER stored in VS Code settings (settings.json is readable by all extensions). Keys are stored using the VS Code SecretStorage API (vscode.ExtensionContext.secrets) which delegates to the OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux libsecret). The extension's activate() reads keys from SecretStorage only when a run starts, passes them to the engine's in-memory provider registry, and clears them from the registry when the run completes. Keys are never written into workflow YAML files, never sent to the desktop app over IPC, never included in run trace exports. When the desktop app is running (hybrid mode), the extension requests key access via the IPC protocol's authenticated key-fetch endpoint — the desktop app returns the key only after verifying the requesting process is the VS Code extension via the shared IPC auth token (a random 32-byte token written to .agentorg/.ipc-token at desktop app startup, readable only by the OS user running the desktop app). Additional: The extension declares a minimal activationEvents list to avoid loading in unrelated workspaces. It activates only when a .agentorg/ folder is detected in the workspace (workspaceContains:.agentorg). This prevents the extension from consuming memory in every open project."
differentiators: Zero-dependency execution: the bundled workflow engine runs entirely in the VS Code extension host Node.js process — no Docker, no desktop app required, no port conflicts. Developers on a fresh machine can install the extension and run their first workflow in under 60 seconds.

Native diff review flow: agent-proposed code changes use VS Code's built-in diff editor, not a custom webview. Developers review proposals with the exact same UI they use for git diff, with per-hunk accept/reject via CodeLens. This is the trust primitive that makes the extension safe for production code.

Shared engine package with desktop and CLI: the workflow engine is a single TypeScript package consumed by all three surfaces. A bug fix or new node type in the engine benefits all three surfaces simultaneously. There is no 'extension-specific' execution path that diverges from the desktop app's behavior.

LSP-powered .agentorg/ editing: the language server makes editing workflow YAML a first-class development experience — autocomplete, hover docs, go-to-definition, and real-time error detection. Developers who write workflows by hand (rather than using the visual canvas) get IDE-quality assistance.

Human-gate approval is ambient: gates surface in the status bar, sidebar, and chat panel simultaneously. A developer who triggers a workflow and returns to coding will see the approval prompt in the status bar without losing their place in the code.

Open extension API: other extensions can subscribe to agentorg.onRunCompleted, intercept agentorg.onFilePatchProposed to add custom validation, or trigger workflows programmatically via agentorg.runWorkflow. This enables the ecosystem — a hypothetical 'AgentOrg + GitHub Actions' extension could trigger a CI workflow every time a run completes successfully.

sharedCore.workflowYAMLSpec:
# Workflow YAML Specification v1.0
# Schema: https://agentflow.dev/schemas/workflow/v1

# Top-level workflow definition
workflow:
  id: string                    # unique, kebab-case
  version: semver               # '1.0.0'
  name: string
  description: string
  tags: string[]
  trigger:                      # what initiates this workflow
    type: manual | webhook | schedule | file_change | mcp_call
    schedule?: cron_expression  # only when type=schedule
    webhook?: { path: string, secret_env: string }
    file_change?: { glob: string, debounce_ms: number }
  inputs:                       # typed input declarations
    - name: string
      type: string | number | boolean | file_path | code_diff | secret
      required: boolean
      default?: any
      description: string
  agents: AgentRef[]            # inline or $ref to agent files
  nodes: Node[]                 # execution graph nodes
  edges: Edge[]                 # directed connections between nodes
  context:                      # shared variables available via {{ctx.*}}
    - key: string
      value: any | '{{interpolation}}'

# Node types
Node:
  id: string
  type: agent | human_gate | condition | transform | parallel | merge
  # --- agent node ---
  agent_ref?: string            # references agents[] by id
  prompt_template?: string      # supports {{variable}} interpolation
  tools?: string[]              # tool ids available to this agent
  model?: string                # override model for this node
  temperature?: number
  max_tokens?: number
  timeout_ms?: number
  retry?: { max: number, backoff: 'linear' | 'exponential' }
  # --- human_gate node ---
  gate_type?: 'approval' | 'input' | 'review'
  assignee?: string | '{{ctx.pr_author}}'
  message_template?: string
  timeout_action?: 'reject' | 'approve' | 'escalate'
  timeout_ms?: number
  # --- condition node ---
  condition?: string            # JS expression over {{run.outputs}}
  branches?: { label: string, condition: string, target_node: string }[]
  # --- transform node ---
  transform?: string            # JS expression, output becomes node result
  # --- parallel node ---
  parallel_of?: string[]        # node ids to run concurrently
  # --- merge node ---
  merge_strategy?: 'concat' | 'object_merge' | 'first' | 'custom'
  merge_fn?: string             # JS expression when strategy=custom

Edge:
  from: string                  # node id
  to: string                    # node id
  label?: string
  condition?: string            # optional JS expression, edge taken when truthy

AgentRef:
  id: string
  name: string
  description: string
  model: string                 # e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6', 'gpt-4o'
  system_prompt: string         # supports {{variable}} interpolation
  tools: ToolDeclaration[]
  mcp_servers?: McpServerRef[]
  memory?: { type: 'none' | 'window' | 'summary', window_size?: number }

ToolDeclaration:
  id: string
  type: builtin | mcp | plugin | http
  # builtin: read_file, write_file, bash, web_search, etc.
  # mcp: references an MCP server tool
  # plugin: user-supplied package
  # http: arbitrary REST endpoint
  config?: Record<string, any>

McpServerRef:
  id: string
  transport: stdio | sse | websocket
  command?: string              # stdio: path to MCP server binary
  args?: string[]
  url?: string                  # sse/websocket
  env?: Record<string, string>  # env vars injected into MCP server process
sharedCore.completeWorkflowExample:
# security-review.workflow.yaml
workflow:
  id: security-review
  version: 1.0.0
  name: AI Security Review
  description: |
    Runs a multi-agent security review on a git diff or file set.
    Triage agent filters noise, then specialist agents review in parallel,
    a synthesis agent compiles findings, and a human gate approves before
    the final report is posted to the PR.
  tags: [security, code-review, ci]
  trigger:
    type: webhook
    webhook:
      path: /hooks/security-review
      secret_env: WEBHOOK_SECRET

  inputs:
    - name: git_diff
      type: code_diff
      required: true
      description: The unified diff to review
    - name: repo_url
      type: string
      required: true
    - name: pr_number
      type: number
      required: false
    - name: pr_author
      type: string
      required: false
    - name: severity_threshold
      type: string
      required: false
      default: medium
      description: Minimum severity to surface — low/medium/high/critical

  context:
    - key: review_date
      value: '{{env.TODAY}}'
    - key: model_fast
      value: claude-haiku-4
    - key: model_smart
      value: claude-sonnet-4-6

  agents:
    - id: triage-agent
      name: Security Triage
      description: Quickly classifies which changed files/lines warrant deep review
      model: '{{ctx.model_fast}}'
      system_prompt: |
        You are a security triage specialist. Given a git diff, identify which
        changed files and line ranges are most likely to contain security issues.
        Categories: injection, auth, crypto, supply_chain, secrets, iac, logic.
        Output JSON: { files: [{path, category, reason, priority: 1-5}] }
      tools: []
      memory:
        type: none

    - id: sast-agent
      name: SAST Analyst
      description: Static analysis for injections, XSS, SSRF, path traversal, etc.
      model: '{{ctx.model_smart}}'
      system_prompt: |
        You are an expert in static application security testing. Focus on:
        injection flaws (SQL, command, LDAP), XSS, SSRF, path traversal,
        deserialization, and insecure direct object references.
        For each finding: { id, severity, cwe_id, file, line_start, line_end,
        description, recommendation, confidence: low|medium|high }
      tools:
        - id: read_file
          type: builtin
        - id: grep
          type: builtin
      mcp_servers:
        - id: filesystem
          transport: stdio
          command: npx
          args: ['-y', '@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem', '{{inputs.repo_url}}']
      memory:
        type: window
        window_size: 20

    - id: secrets-agent
      name: Secrets Scanner
      description: Detects hardcoded credentials, tokens, keys, and private data
      model: '{{ctx.model_fast}}'
      system_prompt: |
        You are a secrets detection specialist. Scan for: API keys, passwords,
        private keys, tokens, connection strings, and PII. Flag any entropy
        anomalies or patterns matching known secret formats.
        Output: [{ id, severity, type, file, line, masked_value, recommendation }]
      tools:
        - id: read_file
          type: builtin
      memory:
        type: none

    - id: dependency-agent
      name: Dependency Auditor
      description: Reviews changes to package manifests for vulnerable dependencies
      model: '{{ctx.model_fast}}'
      system_prompt: |
        You are a supply-chain security specialist. Examine changes to
        package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, Cargo.toml, pom.xml.
        Check for: known CVEs, suspicious new packages, version downgrades,
        typosquatting, and removed lock file integrity.
        Output: [{ id, severity, package, version, cve_ids, recommendation }]
      tools:
        - id: web_search
          type: builtin
          config:
            allowed_domains: [osv.dev, nvd.nist.gov, github.com/advisories]
      memory:
        type: none

    - id: synthesis-agent
      name: Security Report Synthesizer
      description: Merges all specialist findings into a unified, prioritized report
      model: '{{ctx.model_smart}}'
      system_prompt: |
        You are a principal security engineer writing an executive security review.
        Given findings from SAST, secrets, and dependency agents, produce:
        1. Executive summary (2-3 sentences)
        2. Critical & high findings (detailed, actionable)
        3. Medium findings (grouped by category)
        4. Informational notes
        5. Recommended remediation order
        Filter out findings below severity: {{inputs.severity_threshold}}.
        Format as Markdown suitable for a GitHub PR comment.
      tools: []
      memory:
        type: none

    - id: pr-comment-agent
      name: PR Comment Poster
      description: Posts the approved security report to the pull request
      model: '{{ctx.model_fast}}'
      system_prompt: |
        Post the security review report to PR #{{inputs.pr_number}} in
        {{inputs.repo_url}}. Use the GitHub API tool to create a PR review
        with line-level annotations where possible.
      tools:
        - id: github_pr_review
          type: plugin
          config:
            token_env: GITHUB_TOKEN
      memory:
        type: none

  nodes:
    - id: triage
      type: agent
      agent_ref: triage-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Triage the following git diff for security-relevant changes.
        Diff:
        {{inputs.git_diff}}

    - id: parallel-review
      type: parallel
      parallel_of: [sast-review, secrets-review, dependency-review]

    - id: sast-review
      type: agent
      agent_ref: sast-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Perform SAST on these prioritized files from the diff.
        Triage output: {{nodes.triage.output}}
        Full diff:
        {{inputs.git_diff}}

    - id: secrets-review
      type: agent
      agent_ref: secrets-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Scan for secrets and credentials in this diff.
        Diff:
        {{inputs.git_diff}}

    - id: dependency-review
      type: agent
      agent_ref: dependency-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Audit dependency changes in this diff.
        Diff:
        {{inputs.git_diff}}

    - id: merge-findings
      type: merge
      merge_strategy: object_merge

    - id: synthesize
      type: agent
      agent_ref: synthesis-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Synthesize these findings into a final report.
        SAST findings: {{nodes.sast-review.output}}
        Secrets findings: {{nodes.secrets-review.output}}
        Dependency findings: {{nodes.dependency-review.output}}

    - id: has-critical-findings
      type: condition
      condition: 'JSON.parse(nodes["synthesize"].output).critical_count > 0'
      branches:
        - label: has_criticals
          condition: 'JSON.parse(nodes["synthesize"].output).critical_count > 0'
          target_node: human-approval
        - label: no_criticals
          condition: 'JSON.parse(nodes["synthesize"].output).critical_count === 0'
          target_node: auto-post

    - id: human-approval
      type: human_gate
      gate_type: approval
      assignee: '{{inputs.pr_author}}'
      message_template: |
        Security review found {{nodes.synthesize.output.critical_count}} critical
        finding(s) in PR #{{inputs.pr_number}}. Please review and approve or
        reject posting this security report.
        Preview:
        {{nodes.synthesize.output.summary}}
      timeout_ms: 86400000
      timeout_action: escalate

    - id: auto-post
      type: agent
      agent_ref: pr-comment-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Post this approved security review to the PR.
        Report: {{nodes.synthesize.output}}

    - id: approved-post
      type: agent
      agent_ref: pr-comment-agent
      prompt_template: |
        Post this human-approved security review to the PR.
        Report: {{nodes.synthesize.output}}
        Approved by: {{nodes.human-approval.approved_by}}

  edges:
    - from: triage
      to: parallel-review
    - from: parallel-review
      to: merge-findings
    - from: merge-findings
      to: synthesize
    - from: synthesize
      to: has-critical-findings
    - from: has-critical-findings
      to: human-approval
      label: has_criticals
    - from: has-critical-findings
      to: auto-post
      label: no_criticals
    - from: human-approval
      to: approved-post
      condition: 'nodes["human-approval"].decision === "approved"'

localVsCloudMode.phase1Architecture:
Phase 1 — Local Execution Mode:

All LLM API calls originate from the user's machine directly to providers
(Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.). API keys are stored in the local OS
keychain (Keychain on macOS, Credential Manager on Windows, libsecret on Linux).
Run state is persisted to a local SQLite database at
~/.agentflow/runs.db (or %APPDATA%\agentflow\runs.db on Windows).
MCP server processes are spawned as child processes by the engine.
Workflow files are read from the local filesystem.
No data leaves the machine except to LLM API providers.

Local storage layout:
~/.agentflow/
  runs.db                   # SQLite: all run state, events, outputs
  workflows/                # cached/imported workflow definitions
  plugins/                  # installed plugin packages
  mcp-servers/              # managed MCP server binaries
  logs/                     # rolling logs per run

engine is created with:
  createEngine({
    executionMode: 'local',
    apiKeys: { anthropic: keychain.get('ANTHROPIC_API_KEY') },
    storageDir: '~/.agentflow'
  })
localVsCloudMode.phase2Architecture:
Phase 2 — Cloud Execution Mode:

The engine acts as a thin client. When engine.run() is called:
1. Engine serializes the workflow + inputs + user identity to the cloud API.
2. Cloud workers (Kubernetes jobs or serverless functions) execute the run.
3. Engine receives a runId and subscribes to an SSE stream for events.
4. The same RunHandle and RunEvent types are returned — surfaces are unaware
   of the execution location change.
5. Human gate pauses are stored in cloud; any surface (including web portal)
   can fulfill them.
6. LLM API calls are made from cloud workers using provider API keys managed
   by the platform (user links their keys in the portal, or uses platform-
   provisioned keys under the Pro/Enterprise plan).
7. Outputs and run state are stored in cloud; optionally replicated locally.

Cloud engine creation:
  createEngine({
    executionMode: 'cloud',
    cloudEndpoint: 'https://api.agentflow.dev',
    cloudAuthToken: authBridge.getToken(),  // from web portal auth
    telemetry: { reportUsage: true }        // drives portal quota tracking
  })
localVsCloudMode.transparentSwitch:
Transparent mode switching:

The engine exposes an identical TypeScript interface regardless of mode.
Surfaces do not check executionMode — they call engine.run() and handle
RunEvent objects the same way in both modes.

Mode is resolved at engine creation time from:
  1. Explicit config.executionMode override
  2. Presence of a valid cloudAuthToken (implies cloud mode)
  3. User preference stored in ~/.agentflow/settings.json
  4. Default: 'local'

Gradual migration path:
- User starts on local mode (Phase 1).
- User signs up on web portal, gets auth token.
- Desktop app detects token, shows opt-in prompt: 'Run in cloud for
  persistence, team sharing, and no local API key management?'
- User opts in: engine is recreated with executionMode='cloud'.
- CLI: agentflow config set execution-mode cloud
- VS Code: setting agentflow.executionMode = 'cloud'
- All surfaces pick up the change from ~/.agentflow/settings.json
  on next engine initialization — no code changes in surfaces required.

Fallback behavior:
- If cloud is unreachable and executionMode='cloud', engine throws
  CloudUnavailableError with a suggestion to switch to local mode.
- Engine never silently falls back from cloud to local — that could
  leak credentials or bypass enterprise controls.

sharedCore.mcpIntegration:
MCP Integration Architecture:

--- Agents as MCP Servers (outbound) ---
Every loaded workflow can be exposed as an MCP server so that external
tools (Claude Desktop, Cursor, other MCP clients) can invoke your agents.

The engine ships an MCP adapter:
  import { createMcpAdapter } from '@agentflow/core/mcp';
  const adapter = createMcpAdapter(engine, {
    workflows: ['security-review', 'refactor-agent'],
    transport: 'stdio'  // or 'sse'
  });
  adapter.listen(); // registers each workflow as an MCP tool

Each workflow appears as an MCP tool named agentflow_{workflow_id}.
Tool inputSchema is derived from the workflow's inputs[] declarations.
The MCP tool call blocks until run:completed, then returns the outputs.
Human gates emit a special MCP notification asking the client to prompt
the user — this bridges MCP's request-response model with human gates.

--- Agents Consuming MCP Tools ---
Agents declare mcp_servers in their definition. The engine:
1. Spawns (stdio) or connects to (SSE/WS) each MCP server.
2. Calls tools/list on each server and registers discovered tools into
   the agent's tool namespace as mcp_{server_id}_{tool_name}.
3. When an agent calls a tool, the engine routes to the correct MCP server
   using the registered mapping.
4. Tool results are streamed back as AgentToolResultEvents.
5. MCP server processes are kept alive for the run duration, then torn down.

Built-in MCP servers available out-of-the-box (auto-installed via npx):
- @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem  (read/write local files)
- @modelcontextprotocol/server-brave-search (web search)
- @modelcontextprotocol/server-puppeteer   (browser automation)
- @modelcontextprotocol/server-github      (GitHub API)
- @modelcontextprotocol/server-postgres    (database access)

--- MCP Tool Discovery ---
At agent startup the engine performs discovery:
1. Static discovery: tools listed in mcp_servers[].tools_whitelist YAML
   are pre-resolved at workflow load time — fast, deterministic.
2. Dynamic discovery: if no whitelist, the engine calls tools/list at
   agent init time and registers all available tools.
3. Capability caching: tool lists are cached per (server_command, args)
   hash for 1 hour to avoid repeated process spawning.
4. Conflict resolution: if two MCP servers expose a tool with the same
   name, the engine prefixes with the server id: github_create_issue vs
   jira_create_issue. Workflow YAML can use either name.
5. Schema validation: engine validates all MCP tool calls against the
   server-reported JSON schema before sending — prevents malformed calls.

webPortal.purpose: The web portal is the control plane, not the execution plane. It is the single surface where users manage identity, billing, quota, team access, audit compliance, and (in Phase 2) cloud execution queue. Workflows are never designed or run here — that happens in the desktop app, VS Code extension, or CLI. The portal exists to answer: Who is running what, how much does it cost, who has access, and is it compliant?

authBridge.method: OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628), supplemented by long-lived API tokens for CI/CD
authBridge.reasoning: License keys are the simplest but give no revocation, no per-device audit trail, and no user identity — problematic for Enterprise SSO and team workspaces. API tokens work well for CI/CD but are awkward for interactive desktop/CLI auth (user must copy-paste into a file). OAuth Device Flow is the best fit because: (1) the desktop app and CLI can initiate auth with a single command without opening a browser themselves, (2) user authenticates in any browser including their corporate SSO-connected browser, (3) access tokens are short-lived with refresh tokens, enabling revocation, (4) each device gets its own token — revoking one device doesn't affect others. GitHub CLI uses exactly this model for the same reasons. API tokens are issued separately for CI/CD pipelines (non-interactive) and are scoped to specific permissions.
authBridge.flow:
1. Surface calls POST /auth/device — receives device_code + user_code + verification_uri.
2. Surface prints: 'Open https://agentflow.dev/activate and enter code: XXXX-XXXX'.
3. User opens browser, signs in (username/password, Google OAuth, or Enterprise SAML/OIDC).
4. User enters user_code on activation page.
5. Surface polls POST /auth/device/token every 5 seconds with device_code.
6. On success: receives access_token (1hr TTL) + refresh_token (90-day TTL).
7. Tokens stored in OS keychain (never in plaintext files).
8. Engine EngineConfig.cloudAuthToken is set to the access token.
9. CLI: agentflow auth login — runs the full device flow in ~10 seconds.
10. CI/CD: agentflow auth login --with-token — reads AGENTFLOW_TOKEN env var, skips device flow.
11. Token refresh happens transparently inside the engine before API calls.

syncArchitecture.syncMechanism: Opt-in sync per data category. Nothing syncs by default in local mode. Sync is enabled per-category in settings (desktop app or CLI: agentflow config set sync.run_history true). Sync is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.3) and at rest (AES-256). Sync is eventually consistent — the portal is never the source of truth for local execution; it is a read-only aggregate view of what surfaces choose to report. In Phase 2 (cloud execution), run state lives in the cloud natively and sync is no longer needed for that category.
whatSyncs: Usage telemetry (tokens per model per agent per run) — opt-in, anonymizable; this is what drives the portal quota dashboard. Reported as compact usage events at run completion, not full transcripts.
Run metadata (runId, workflowId, status, duration, timestamps, tags) — opt-in; enables run history view in portal without uploading outputs.
Workflow definitions (YAML/JSON) — explicit cloud backup action by user, or team-share action; never automatic background sync.
Agent library — team-level sharing; when a user publishes an agent to team workspace it is uploaded; pull is explicit (agentflow agent pull <id>).

Audit log events (enterprise only) — structured event records for compliance: who ran what workflow, what human gates were fulfilled by whom, what tools were called. Pushed from engine at run completion.
syncArchitecture.dataPrivacy: Full LLM prompt/response transcripts are NEVER synced to the portal in any tier or any mode — they remain on the user's machine (local mode) or in the isolated cloud worker's ephemeral storage (cloud mode, deleted after run). The portal sees only structured metadata and aggregate token counts, not content. This is the core privacy guarantee that makes the platform viable for enterprise security teams running sensitive code reviews.

phaseTransition.phase1Summary: Phase 1 delivers a fully functional local-execution platform. The core engine is open-source (Apache 2.0) and runs entirely on the user's machine. LLM API calls go directly to providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.) using keys the user controls. Run state is persisted in a local SQLite database. The web portal exists but is lightweight: it only receives opt-in usage telemetry pushed at run completion, displays the quota/usage dashboard, and manages human gate notifications. No code runs in the cloud in Phase 1 — the portal is purely a metrics aggregator and control plane. This architecture lets the platform launch without cloud infrastructure for execution, dramatically reducing operational complexity and earning trust with security-conscious users.
phaseTransition.phase2Trigger: Phase 2 cloud execution is triggered by one or more of: (a) user explicitly opts in via 'agentflow config set execution-mode cloud' or the desktop app settings toggle, (b) a workflow YAML declares 'execution: cloud' at the top level (useful for CI/CD workflows that should not depend on a local machine being online), or (c) an Enterprise admin enforces cloud mode for all team members via workspace policy. The opt-in is per-surface or per-workflow — a developer might keep local mode in VS Code for interactive work but set cloud mode in CI/CD. The engine interface is identical in both modes; surfaces require zero code changes.
phaseTransition.cloudArchitecture:
Cloud execution architecture (Phase 2):

Control Plane (portal backend):
- Run submission API: POST /runs — accepts workflow + inputs, queues a job, returns runId
- Run state API: GET /runs/{runId} — current state, outputs, events replay
- SSE endpoint: GET /runs/{runId}/events — real-time event stream for active runs
- Gate API: POST /runs/{runId}/gates/{gateId}/decide — fulfill human gates from any surface
- Worker orchestration: Kubernetes Job per run, auto-scaled 0-N
- Run state store: PostgreSQL (structured) + S3 (large outputs, artifacts)
- Event bus: Redis Streams for real-time event fan-out to connected SSE clients
- LLM API key management: user-linked keys stored in HashiCorp Vault, injected into worker environment at runtime

Data Plane (cloud workers):
- Isolated Kubernetes Jobs (one pod per run, network policy restricts egress to allowed LLM providers + declared MCP server URLs)
- Same packages/core engine code runs inside workers — no separate cloud-specific engine
- MCP servers run as sidecar containers within the same pod
- Worker ephemeral storage: all LLM transcripts deleted on pod termination
- Output artifacts uploaded to user-scoped S3 prefix, deleted after 30 days (configurable)
- Enterprise: dedicated node pools, optional VPC peering to customer cloud account

Client-side in cloud mode:
- engine.run() → HTTP POST to cloud API → returns RunHandle backed by SSE subscription
- RunHandle.events AsyncIterable yields the same RunEvent types, now delivered over SSE
- Human gates in cloud: HumanGatePausedEvent delivered to all surfaces + notification channels simultaneously; first surface to call engine.resume() wins (idempotent)
- Offline handling: if SSE connection drops, client reconnects using Last-Event-ID header and replays missed events from the event bus
- Local fallback: if cloudEndpoint unreachable, engine throws — surfaces show 'Cloud unavailable, switch to local mode?' prompt

Migration of existing local runs:
- Local runs (Phase 1) stay in local SQLite — no migration needed
- Portal shows a unified run history combining local-reported runs and cloud-native runs, distinguished by a 'surface' badge
phaseTransition.migrationPath:
Migration path from Phase 1 to Phase 2 for a typical user:

Week 1 (Phase 1): User installs desktop app. Creates workflows, runs them locally. API keys in OS keychain. Run history in local SQLite.

Month 1: User signs up on web portal. Desktop app detects auth token, shows 'Connect to portal?' prompt. User opts in — usage telemetry starts flowing to portal dashboard. Execution remains local.

Month 2: User wants team sharing. Upgrades to Pro. Publishes a few agents to cloud backup. Enables 'agentflow config set sync.run_history true'. Portal now shows full run history. Execution still local.

Month 3: User tries cloud execution for a CI/CD workflow that needs to run without a local machine. Sets 'execution: cloud' in that specific workflow YAML. One workflow runs in cloud; all others still local. Human gates for that workflow now appear in portal /gates page.

Month 6: Enterprise team onboards. Admin enforces cloud mode in workspace policy. All team members' runs now execute in cloud. Usage tracked per-member in portal. SSO configured. Audit logs enabled. Individual developers never needed to change any code — only the EngineConfig.executionMode changed, transparently applied by the workspace policy sync.

goToMarket.phase1LaunchStrategy: Lead with the VS Code extension on the VS Code Marketplace as the zero-friction entry point — it reaches developers in their existing environment with no behavior change required. Seed the .agentorg/ workflow library on GitHub before launch with 20–30 high-quality workflow templates covering the top developer use cases (code review, PR description generation, test generation, dependency audit, documentation generation). These templates serve as both distribution and product education.
