goToMarket.viralMechanic: Workflow sharing is the core viral loop. When a developer commits a .agentorg/workflow.yaml file to their team's repository, every other developer on the team encounters the file in git, installs the extension to run it, and becomes a user. The workflow file itself is the invite. Secondary viral mechanic: the public workflow library on GitHub — developers star, fork, and PR improve workflows, generating organic GitHub activity and SEO. Tertiary: CLI output includes a shareable workflow run summary link (web portal) that developers paste into Slack or PR comments, exposing non-users to the product.

workflowAsCodeDifferentiator.keyInsight: By making workflows YAML files that live in .agentorg/ inside the project repository, the product transforms from a personal productivity tool into a team infrastructure primitive — the workflow file becomes the durable, reviewable, auditable record of how a team uses AI, which no conversational or session-based tool can provide. This single design decision creates switching costs, team network effects, and an enterprise compliance story simultaneously.
useCases: Team onboarding: new engineers run the team's established AI workflows on day one without learning prompting — the workflow encodes the team's best practices
CI/CD parity: the same PR review workflow a developer runs locally in VS Code runs automatically in GitHub Actions on every pull request — one workflow definition, two execution contexts
Workflow code review: workflows are reviewed in PRs like any other code change, creating a governance and quality control layer for AI usage that no other tool provides
Audit trail: git history of .agentorg/ files shows exactly how the team's AI workflows evolved over time — valuable for compliance, post-mortems, and knowledge transfer
Open-source workflow library: teams publish their .agentorg/ directories to GitHub, creating an ecosystem of community-maintained workflow templates (analogous to GitHub Actions marketplace)
Workflow versioning and rollback: if a workflow change causes regressions in AI output quality, git revert fixes it — no vendor dashboard required
Monorepo workflow inheritance: parent directory workflows are inherited by subdirectories, enabling org-level standards with project-level overrides
Documentation artifact: the workflow YAML file documents not just what AI does but why — comments in YAML serve as living documentation of the team's AI strategy
workflowAsCodeDifferentiator.networkEffects: Each workflow file committed to a shared repo creates a pull toward adoption for every team member who pulls that repo. At the org level, a platform team publishing canonical workflows to an internal GitHub repository creates a hub-and-spoke distribution model where the platform team's workflow quality improvements propagate to all consuming teams automatically via git pull. This is a network effect that no session-based tool (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline) can replicate — their value is personal and does not compound with team size. Our value scales with team size because each new team member both consumes existing workflows and contributes new ones.

Workflow approval workflows (PR-style review before team publishing)
SSO via Google and GitHub OAuth
Team onboarding workflow templates
Slack and GitHub integration for workflow run notifications
Dedicated support with 48-hour response SLA
Everything in Team for all seats
SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning
SOC 2 Type II compliance documentation
Audit logs for all workflow runs and modifications
Custom model routing rules (route specific workflows to approved models only)
Air-gapped deployment option (on-premises web portal)
Dedicated customer success manager
SLA with 99.9% uptime guarantee for cloud execution (Phase 2)
Custom workflow template development (professional services)
Private workflow marketplace for internal distribution
4-hour support response SLA

pricingStrategy.rationale: The free tier is deliberately generous on capability (full canvas, full CLI, full extension) but gated on collaboration and scale — a solo developer gets the full power of the platform indefinitely, which drives deep adoption and word-of-mouth. Monetization happens when workflows move from personal use to team infrastructure, which is when the product delivers its highest value. This avoids the failure mode of gating core features behind payment (which kills adoption) while ensuring that the highest-value use cases (team workflow libraries, centralized governance, CI/CD at scale) are firmly in paid tiers. We do not compete with LLM providers on API pricing — users bring their own keys on Free and Pro, and managed key pooling on Team/Enterprise is a convenience feature priced for its operational value, not as a margin play on token costs.

cloudExecutionTrigger.businessCase: Cloud execution in Phase 2 is not a technical milestone — it is a business model unlock. Local-first in Phase 1 establishes trust, removes the data-leaving-the-machine objection, and allows rapid adoption without infrastructure cost. Cloud execution becomes the right investment when the team workflow library adoption creates demand for workflows that must run without a human's machine being on: scheduled automation, triggered pipelines, mobile-initiated runs, and always-on monitoring agents. At that point, cloud execution is not a nice-to-have — it is the only way to deliver the value the product has already trained users to expect.
cloudOnlyCapabilities: Scheduled workflow execution (cron-triggered agents that run at 2am without a laptop being open)
Webhook-triggered workflows (GitHub push triggers a workflow run without developer action)
Always-on monitoring agents (continuously watching a service and alerting on anomalies)
Mobile triggering (initiate a workflow from a phone — impossible with local-only execution)
Team workflow runs without VPN (remote team members trigger shared workflows without connecting to company network)
Parallel cloud-scale execution (run 50 agent instances simultaneously for large codebase analysis — impossible on a laptop)
Workflow run sharing with full replay (share a cloud run URL with a manager or stakeholder who has no local setup)
Cross-team workflow orchestration (a platform team's cloud workflow triggers downstream team workflows without any human in the loop)
Persistent agent memory across sessions (cloud execution enables durable agent state that survives laptop restarts)
Compliance and audit logging to immutable cloud storage (required for regulated industries)
cloudExecutionTrigger.whenToLaunch: Launch Phase 2 cloud execution when three conditions are simultaneously true: (1) at least 500 teams have active .agentorg/ workflow files in shared repositories, indicating the workflow-as-code primitive has been validated; (2) at least 20% of Pro/Team users have requested or asked about scheduled or webhook-triggered execution in support tickets or feature requests, indicating demand is pull-based not push-based; and (3) the core local execution runtime is stable enough that cloud execution is a deployment environment change, not an architectural rewrite. The business signal is that users are leaving workflows on their desktops overnight wishing they ran automatically — that is the moment cloud execution sells itself.

productIdentity.name: Flowgent
productIdentity.tagline: Design agents. Ship workflows. Own every run.
productIdentity.oneLinePitch: Flowgent is the multi-surface AI agent workflow platform that lets developers and teams visually design, version-control, and execute multi-model agent pipelines — from a desktop canvas, VS Code, the CLI, or the cloud — with every run debuggable, every cost tracked, and every workflow exportable as a git-friendly YAML file.

technologyChoices.sharedCoreLanguage: TypeScript — one language, one type system (Zod schemas shared end-to-end), one CI chain. No Python runtime alongside Node; all LLM orchestration is handled by @langchain/langgraph (langgraph.js) + Vercel AI SDK + provider SDKs (anthropic, openai, @google/generative-ai, deepseek). Bun is the worker runtime for agent node execution threads.
technologyChoices.desktopFramework: Tauri v2 — 2–5 MB bundle vs Electron's 85–120 MB; tauri-plugin-fs, tauri-plugin-shell, tauri-plugin-sql (SQLite), tauri-plugin-tray, tauri-plugin-global-shortcut cover all five non-negotiables (filesystem, child process, local HTTP, SQLite, system tray). The ReactFlow canvas runs identically in the OS WebView (WKWebView/WebView2) as it would in a browser. Rust surface is limited to system-level glue; all business logic stays in TypeScript.
technologyChoices.vscodeExtensionModel: Standard VS Code Extension API (contributes.menus, StatusBarItem, WebviewPanel, TreeDataProvider). The extension runs a bundled in-process variant of the shared core engine (Model C hybrid) so workflows execute locally without requiring the desktop app to be running. Webpack bundles the extension to a single CJS file; the WebviewPanel hosts a Vite-built React panel.
technologyChoices.cliLanguage: TypeScript compiled with tsup to a single ESM bundle, distributed via npm as 'flowgent' (global install). Uses commander.js for argument parsing, ink (React for CLIs) for interactive TUI output, and @clack/prompts for setup wizards. The CLI invokes the same shared @flowgent/core engine as all other surfaces.
technologyChoices.portalStack: Vite + React SPA (not Next.js). Justification: the canvas is client-centric (ReactFlow state, SSE EventSource connections, Zustand stores), SSR adds zero value (auth-gated, no SEO), and Next.js App Router RSC boundaries create friction with persistent SSE connections and complex canvas state. TanStack Router for typed file-based routing, TanStack Query for server state, Zustand + immer for client state. Backend: Hono (TypeScript) on Node.js/Bun, deployed separately. shadcn/ui + Radix primitives + Tailwind CSS for UI.
technologyChoices.localDatabase: Phase 1 (local-only): SQLite via tauri-plugin-sql (desktop) and better-sqlite3 (CLI/extension). Schema managed by Drizzle ORM with migration files checked into git. Phase 2 (cloud): PostgreSQL 16 + Redis 7. BullMQ on Redis for job queues. Drizzle ORM used for both SQLite and Postgres — identical query API, only the driver changes.
technologyChoices.apiKeyStorage: Phase 1 — desktop: OS keychain via tauri-plugin-keychain (macOS Keychain, Windows Credential Manager, Linux libsecret). CLI: OS keychain via keytar. VS Code extension: vscode.SecretStorage API. Never written to disk in plaintext. Phase 2 — cloud portal: AES-256-GCM encryption at rest in Postgres (bytea column), encryption key from environment secret manager (Doppler/Infisecret). Key hint (last 4 chars) stored plaintext for display. Keys are decrypted in-process at call time, never logged, never in Redis job payloads.

monoRepoStructure.layout: Turborepo monorepo with pnpm workspaces. All packages use TypeScript with shared tsconfig bases. Turborepo remote cache for CI. Single root package.json for tooling (ESLint, Prettier, Vitest config). Three app surfaces and five shared packages.

packages.purpose: Tauri v2 desktop application. src-tauri/ contains Rust Tauri commands and plugin configuration. src/ contains the React + Vite frontend (WorkflowCanvas, ReactFlow nodes, Zustand stores, SSE manager, all UI panels). Calls @flowgent/core via Tauri invoke IPC for engine operations; calls OS APIs (keychain, filesystem, tray) via Tauri plugin IPC.

VS Code extension. src/extension.ts is the activation entry point. src/engine/ contains the bundled in-process agent execution engine (imports @flowgent/core directly). src/panels/ contains WebviewPanel React UIs (Sidebar, HumanGatePanel, RunMonitor). Webpack bundles to out/extension.js (CJS). Published to VS Code Marketplace as 'flowgent.flowgent'.

Terminal CLI. src/index.ts is the commander.js entry. src/commands/ contains subcommands: run, list, create, import, export, status, logs, gate. Uses ink for streaming TUI output (live node status, token stream). Installed globally via npm install -g flowgent. Reads workflows from .flowgent/ directory in the project root or from a path flag.

Cloud web portal (Phase 2). Vite + React SPA. src/routes/ for TanStack Router file-based routes. src/stores/ for Zustand stores. src/components/canvas/ for ReactFlow nodes and edges. Deployed to Cloudflare Pages or Vercel. Calls apps/api over HTTPS.

Cloud backend API (Phase 2). Hono on Bun. src/routes/ for REST endpoints (/workflows, /runs, /agents, /providers, /cost). src/engine/ imports @flowgent/core and wraps it with BullMQ job dispatch and Redis stream SSE. src/workers/ contains BullMQ worker processes (orchestrator-pool, node-pool, system-pool). Postgres via Drizzle ORM, Redis via ioredis. Deployed as a Docker container.

@flowgent/core — the shared execution engine. The single most important package. Exports: WorkflowEngine (parse YAML, build DAG RunPlan, execute nodes, checkpoint state), AgentRunner (invoke LLM via provider adapter, handle tool calls, retry/fallback), WorkflowYAMLParser (parse + validate workflow YAML with Zod), ToolNormalizer (provider-specific tool schema conversion), RunEventBus (EventEmitter for SSE events). Has zero platform-specific imports — runs identically in Tauri WebView, VS Code extension host, Node.js CLI, and Bun API server.

@flowgent/shared — Zod schemas, TypeScript types, and constants shared across all surfaces and packages. WorkflowSchema, AgentSchema, RunSchema, NodeSchema, EdgeSchema, RunEvent, CostEvent, HumanGateEvent. Imported by every other package. No runtime dependencies except zod.

@flowgent/db — Drizzle ORM schema definitions and migration files. Exports separate configs for SQLite (local) and Postgres (cloud) — same table names and column types, different driver. Migration CLI: drizzle-kit generate + drizzle-kit migrate. Used by apps/desktop (SQLite), apps/cli (SQLite), apps/api (Postgres).

@flowgent/llm — LLM provider adapters. ProviderAdapter interface implemented for: AnthropicAdapter (claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4), OpenAIAdapter (gpt-4o, gpt-4.1), GeminiAdapter (gemini-2.5-pro), DeepSeekAdapter. Each adapter normalizes streaming responses, tool calls, and usage tokens to the canonical format defined in @flowgent/shared. ToolNormalizer, CostTracker, RateLimiter, FallbackChain live here.

@flowgent/ui — shared React component library. Contains all ReactFlow custom node types (AgentNode, ConditionNode, FanOutNode, AggregatorNode, LoopNode, HumanGateNode, InputNode, OutputNode, ToolNode) and custom edge types. These components are imported by apps/desktop, apps/vscode-extension panels, and apps/portal — ensuring visual consistency across surfaces. Also contains shadcn/ui base components and Tailwind config.

phase1Architecture.description: Phase 1 is a fully local, zero-cloud-dependency product. The desktop app (Tauri v2) is the primary surface. All data stays on the user's machine. No account, no subscription, no server to run. The engine executes in the Tauri WebView process using @flowgent/core. SQLite (via tauri-plugin-sql) stores workflows, agents, run history, and cost records. API keys live in the OS keychain via tauri-plugin-keychain — never on disk in plaintext. The VS Code extension and CLI ship alongside the desktop app and share the same @flowgent/core engine, so all three surfaces work offline from day one.
whatShips: Tauri v2 desktop app with ReactFlow canvas editor
Local SQLite database for all workflow/agent/run data via Drizzle ORM
OS keychain API key storage (macOS Keychain / Windows Credential Manager / libsecret)
@flowgent/core engine: YAML parsing, DAG execution, checkpoint/resume, retry/fallback
@flowgent/llm adapters for Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek
VS Code extension with inline workflow triggering, status bar monitor, and sidebar panels
ReactFlow canvas with all 9 node types and custom edges
Real-time SSE-style token streaming inside node faces (IPC-based, not HTTP SSE in Phase 1)
Human gate approval flow (modal in desktop, terminal prompt in CLI, WebviewPanel in VS Code)
Per-run cost tracking and per-node token counts stored locally in SQLite
Run history with replay, node inspection, and Gantt trace timeline
Agent library with inline test chat
Starter workflow templates (3–5 curated templates bundled at install)
whatDoesNotShip: Webhook and schedule triggers (file_change trigger ships; webhook/schedule require a cloud listener)
BullMQ/Redis job queue (not needed locally — execution is synchronous in Bun workers managed by @flowgent/core)
phase1Architecture.dataFlow: User action (canvas Run button / CLI flowgent run / VS Code right-click) -> @flowgent/core.WorkflowEngine.start(workflowId, input) -> WorkflowYAMLParser validates YAML -> DAG RunPlan built -> AgentRunner spawns per-node Bun worker_thread (or runs inline for VS Code extension host) -> @flowgent/llm.ProviderAdapter streams tokens via AbortController-guarded fetch -> tokens emitted on RunEventBus -> surface-specific renderer receives events (Tauri emit IPC / VS Code WebviewPanel postMessage / ink TUI update) -> run checkpointed to SQLite on each node completion -> final output written to SQLite run record -> cost event written to SQLite cost_events table

phase2Architecture.description: Phase 2 adds a cloud execution layer and web portal without replacing or breaking the local-first Phase 1 surfaces. The core change is that @flowgent/core can now be invoked by apps/api (Hono + Bun) via BullMQ jobs instead of direct function calls, enabling multi-user access, team workflow sharing, cloud-triggered runs (webhooks, schedules), and the web portal. Existing desktop/CLI/extension users can optionally link a Flowgent Cloud account and push workflows to the cloud for shared access — but they can continue running locally without any account.
cloudComponents: apps/api: Hono REST API on Bun — wraps @flowgent/core with BullMQ job dispatch, Redis Stream SSE, Postgres state, and multi-tenant auth (Clerk or Auth.js with magic link + OAuth)
apps/portal: Vite + React SPA — identical canvas UX to the desktop app, running in a browser instead of Tauri WebView
PostgreSQL 16 (Neon serverless or self-hosted on Railway/Fly.io) — replaces SQLite for cloud runs; Drizzle ORM migration from SQLite schema is ~90% compatible

Redis 7 (Upstash or self-hosted) — BullMQ job queues (orchestrator-pool concurrency=5, node-pool concurrency=20, system-pool concurrency=50) + Redis Streams for SSE log delivery + sliding-window rate limiter sorted sets
BullMQ worker processes (apps/api/src/workers/) — orchestrator-pool, node-pool, system-pool with Bun worker_thread per agent node
Cloudflare Workers or Vercel Edge for webhook listener endpoints (POST /webhooks/:workflowId) — ultra-low-latency enqueue without waking the full API server
Doppler or Infisecret for server-side encryption key management (PROVIDER_KEY_ENCRYPTION_SECRET)
Object storage (R2 or S3) for large run output artifacts (file outputs, generated files from ToolNodes)
whatChanges: API key storage moves from OS keychain to AES-256-GCM encrypted Postgres bytea column for cloud runs; local runs still use OS keychain
Run execution moves from in-process Bun worker_threads to BullMQ jobs dispatched by apps/api, enabling multi-server scaling
SSE delivery moves from Tauri IPC/postMessage to HTTP SSE over Redis Streams (XREAD BLOCK) consumed by apps/portal EventSource
Workflow storage gains multi-tenancy: org_id column on all tables, row-level security in Postgres, team-level sharing permissions
Webhook and schedule triggers become functional: Cloudflare Worker enqueues workflow.start jobs; cron is managed by BullMQ's repeat job system

Human gate notifications gain email/Slack delivery (Phase 2 ops feature): when a gate is pending and the assignee is not actively viewing the portal, a notification is sent via Resend (email) or Slack webhook

The desktop app and CLI gain a 'sync to cloud' command that pushes local SQLite workflows to the cloud Postgres and optionally executes future runs in the cloud — users choose per-workflow whether to run locally or in the cloud
Cost tracking gains org-level aggregate views, budget alerts (POST to Slack/email when monthly spend exceeds threshold), and CSV export

dayOneDX.command: Download Flowgent-1.0.0-aarch64.dmg (macOS, ~4 MB) from flowgent.dev/download or: brew install --cask flowgent
dayOneDX.result: App opens. No account prompt. Canvas is visible but dimmed. Single focused CTA: 'Connect your first API key'.

dayOneDX.command: Click 'Connect API Key' -> select Anthropic -> paste sk-ant-... -> click 'Verify & Save'
dayOneDX.result: Key verified inline with a green checkmark. Model list populates in real time showing claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-opus-4 with context windows and $/token pricing. Key is stored in macOS Keychain — never on disk.

dayOneDX.command: Click 'Create Agent' -> type name 'Summarizer' -> select claude-sonnet-4-6 -> write system prompt 'You summarize text concisely.' -> click 'Test Agent' -> type 'Summarize the history of the internet' -> watch streaming response
dayOneDX.result: Streaming tokens appear in the inline test chat. Agent is saved to local SQLite. User sees their agent is live before touching the canvas.

dayOneDX.command: Click template card 'Research Pipeline' on the dashboard
dayOneDX.result: Canvas opens with a pre-built 3-node workflow: InputNode -> ResearchAgent -> SummaryAgent -> OutputNode. Nodes use placeholder agents that auto-bind to the user's just-created agent.

dayOneDX.command: Click 'Run' button in canvas toolbar (or press Cmd+Enter) -> fill in input modal: 'topic: quantum computing' -> click 'Start Run'
dayOneDX.result: Nodes light up sequentially with pulsing rings. Streaming tokens appear inside the ResearchAgent node face. Run completes in ~15 seconds. OutputNode shows the final summary. Run History panel slides up from the bottom showing: duration 14.2s, cost $0.0031, 1,847 tokens.

dayOneDX.command: In VS Code: Extensions panel -> search 'Flowgent' -> Install. Or: code --install-extension flowgent.flowgent
dayOneDX.result: Flowgent sidebar appears in VS Code Activity Bar. Status bar item shows 'Flowgent: idle' at the bottom right. Right-clicking any .ts/.py/.md file now shows 'Run Flowgent Workflow on this file' in the context menu.

dayOneDX.step: Run a workflow on a file from VS Code
dayOneDX.command: Right-click a TypeScript file -> 'Run Flowgent Workflow on this file' -> select 'Code Review Chain' from QuickPick -> press Enter
dayOneDX.result: Status bar item changes to '$(loading~spin) 1 run active'. Flowgent sidebar opens showing the live run with per-node streaming status. Completed nodes show green checkmarks. Final output appears in the Run Output panel as a formatted code review.

dayOneDX.command: npm install -g flowgent && flowgent run ./workflows/code-review.yaml --input file=./src/index.ts
dayOneDX.result: Terminal shows ink-rendered TUI: animated node names with status indicators (running/done/error), streaming token output for the active node, and a final cost/duration summary line. Exit code 0 on success, 1 on workflow failure — CI-friendly.

killerFeatures.why: Token streaming appears inside individual ReactFlow node faces as the workflow executes — not in a separate log panel, but directly on the canvas node. Parallel branches stream simultaneously. Users watch their multi-agent pipeline think in real time. This transforms the canvas from a static diagram into a live operational display. No competitor (Claude Code, Cursor, n8n, Dify) does this — they all separate the diagram from the output.

Every workflow is a reviewable, diffable, version-controllable YAML file. Teams can PR workflow changes, revert bad agent prompts, and ship workflow changes through the same CI/CD pipeline as code changes. This is the graduation path from Claude Code and Cursor — users who outgrow chat-driven, stateless interactions get persistence and collaboration without leaving their git workflow. Competitor n8n uses proprietary JSON blobs; Dify stores workflows in a database with no git integration.

When a node fails mid-workflow (rate limit, bad output, timeout), users click the red-pulsing failed node and see a 'Retry from here' button. The engine replays from that checkpoint using the already-completed upstream outputs — no re-running the full workflow, no re-spending tokens on steps that already succeeded. Checkpoint-based resume (SQLite Phase 1, Postgres Phase 2) makes this possible. This single feature turns debugging from a destructive, expensive act into a surgical one.

Each agent node can be configured with a fallback chain: [{claude-sonnet-4-6, 3 attempts}, {gpt-4o, 2 attempts}, {gemini-2-5-pro, 1 attempt}]. If the primary model rate-limits or fails, the engine automatically tries the next model in the chain without user intervention. Production workflows keep running even during provider outages. No other visual workflow tool exposes per-node model fallback chains as a first-class UI concept.

killerFeatures.feature: Human gate with timeout and escalation
killerFeatures.why: HumanGateNode pauses any workflow at any point and requires a real human decision (approve/reject/provide input) before continuing. Gates have configurable timeouts with auto-approve or auto-reject fallbacks and optional Slack/email notification (Phase 2). This makes Flowgent viable for compliance-sensitive workflows (content moderation, legal review, financial approvals) where fully autonomous AI execution is unacceptable. This is the feature that sells Flowgent to enterprise teams, not just individual developers.

killerFeatures.feature: Per-node cost waterfall in run history
killerFeatures.why: Every run shows a per-node cost breakdown: which agent consumed how many tokens at what price. Users immediately see that one node accounts for 80% of spend and can swap it for a cheaper model on that node specifically. This granular cost visibility — not just total run cost but per-node, per-model attribution — is unique among workflow tools and directly addresses the top developer anxiety about production LLM usage: unpredictable costs.

Installing the VS Code extension and right-clicking any file to run a workflow on it requires no desktop app, no account, no server. The extension bundles @flowgent/core and runs the workflow in-process. For developers who live in VS Code, this is the path of least resistance to their first meaningful Flowgent run. The install-to-value gap is under 3 minutes.

buildOrder.why: These three packages are the foundation everything else builds on. @flowgent/shared defines the Zod schemas and TypeScript types; @flowgent/llm provides the provider adapters (Anthropic first, then OpenAI, Gemini, DeepSeek); @flowgent/core provides the WorkflowEngine, DAG runner, checkpoint logic, and RunEventBus. Without a working engine, no surface can be built. Build them in this order with 100% unit test coverage (Vitest) before touching any surface code. This also forces the YAML spec to be finalized as a Zod schema, preventing later breaking changes. Target: 2–3 weeks.
buildOrder.milestone: Engine milestone: flowgent run ./workflow.yaml works from a Node.js test script, streaming tokens to console, checkpointing to SQLite, retrying on failure.

buildOrder.why: The CLI is the fastest feedback loop for validating the engine under real conditions. It has no UI complexity (no ReactFlow, no Tauri Rust, no VS Code API) — just ink TUI over @flowgent/core. Building the CLI first forces every engine API to be ergonomic for a scripted caller (no hidden assumptions about a browser environment), which improves the engine's design. The CLI also becomes the integration test harness: flowgent run test-workflow.yaml --input ... becomes the fastest way to regression-test the engine. Target: 1–2 weeks after engine milestone.
buildOrder.milestone: CLI milestone: flowgent run, flowgent list, flowgent logs, flowgent gate commands all work. A 3-node sequential workflow runs end-to-end with streaming output in the terminal.

buildOrder.why: With a proven engine (tested via CLI), the desktop app is about building the canvas UI over a solid foundation. Build packages/ui (all 9 ReactFlow node types) as part of this phase — these components will later be shared with apps/portal. The Tauri Rust layer is thin (keychain IPC, filesystem IPC, tray setup); the bulk of work is the React canvas and Zustand stores. Build in this order within desktop: (1) canvas with node types and edges, (2) Zustand stores and SSE event handling, (3) AgentNode streaming display, (4) human gate modal, (5) run history panel, (6) settings/provider/agent CRUD pages. Target: 4–6 weeks.
buildOrder.milestone: Desktop milestone: a user can install the .dmg, add an API key via macOS Keychain, build a 3-node workflow on the canvas, run it with live streaming, see cost breakdown in run history — all offline, no account.

buildOrder.why: The VS Code extension reuses @flowgent/core (already proven) and packages/ui components (rendered in WebviewPanel). The key engineering work is the VS Code API integration: context menu registration, StatusBarController, TreeDataProvider for the sidebar, and the in-process engine activation. Build after desktop because the Webview panels can share CSS and component patterns from the desktop app's canvas work. The extension is the single highest-leverage distribution channel — VS Code has 15M+ daily users and the Marketplace provides organic discovery. Target: 2–3 weeks.
buildOrder.milestone: Extension milestone: right-click a .ts file -> select workflow -> watch streaming status in sidebar -> see human gate prompt in WebviewPanel -> approve -> run completes.

buildOrder.why: Only build the cloud layer after Phase 1 surfaces are battle-tested by real users. Real usage data from Phase 1 will reveal which workflows users want to share (justifying multi-tenancy), which triggers they actually use (justifying webhook/schedule infra), and where the engine's performance bottlenecks are (justifying BullMQ + Redis). Building cloud prematurely means over-engineering for imagined requirements. The apps/api server largely wraps @flowgent/core with BullMQ dispatch — the engine is already written. apps/portal is largely a web-hosted version of the desktop canvas — packages/ui is already written. The incremental Phase 2 work is primarily auth, multi-tenancy, BullMQ wiring, and deployment infra. Target: 6–8 weeks after Phase 1 ships.
buildOrder.milestone: Cloud milestone: a team can log in to flowgent.dev, import a workflow YAML from git, run it in the cloud, share a run replay URL with a colleague, and approve a human gate via email link.

workflowYAMLSpec.format: YAML 1.2, UTF-8. File extension: .flowgent.yaml or stored in .flowgent/ directory at project root. Schema version is a top-level field and is validated against the @flowgent/shared WorkflowSchema Zod definition on parse. Interpolation uses {{variable}} syntax throughout (inputs, context, prompt templates). Node IDs are kebab-case strings unique within the workflow. Edge sources can reference node output handles by name (nodeId:handleName) for condition/fanout nodes; omitting the handle name defaults to the default output handle.
workflowYAMLSpec.agentExample:
agents:
  - id: summarizer
    name: Summarizer Agent
    model: claude-sonnet-4-6
    provider: anthropic
    system_prompt: |
      You are a concise summarizer. Summarize the input in 3 bullet points.
      Focus on: {{ctx.focus_area}}
    temperature: 0.3
    max_tokens: 512
    tools: []
    retry:
      max: 3
      backoff: exponential
    fallback_chain:
      - model: gpt-4o
        provider: openai
        max_attempts: 2
      - model: gemini-2.5-pro
        provider: google
        max_attempts: 1
workflowYAMLSpec.completeExample:
# .flowgent/code-review-pipeline.flowgent.yaml
schema_version: '1.0'
workflow:
  id: code-review-pipeline
  version: '1.2.0'
  name: Code Review Pipeline
  description: |
    Three-stage code review: security scan, style review, and human approval gate.
  tags: [engineering, review, security]

  trigger:
    type: file_change
    file_change:
      glob: 'src/**/*.ts'
      debounce_ms: 2000

  inputs:
    - name: file_path
      type: file_path
      required: true
      description: Path to the TypeScript file to review
    - name: reviewer_email
      type: string
      required: false
      default: 'team@example.com'
      description: Email to notify when human gate is reached

  context:
    - key: focus_area
      value: 'security vulnerabilities and type safety'
    - key: code_content
      value: '{{inputs.file_path | read_file}}'

  agents:
    - id: security-scanner
      name: Security Scanner
      model: claude-sonnet-4-6
      provider: anthropic
      system_prompt: |
        You are a security-focused code reviewer. Analyze the following TypeScript
        code for security vulnerabilities, injection risks, and unsafe patterns.
        Return a JSON object with fields: {issues: [{severity, line, description, fix}], score: 0-10}
      temperature: 0.1
      max_tokens: 1024
      retry:
        max: 3
        backoff: exponential
      fallback_chain:
        - model: gpt-4o
          provider: openai
          max_attempts: 2

    - id: style-reviewer
      name: Style Reviewer
      model: claude-sonnet-4-6
      provider: anthropic
      system_prompt: |
        You are a TypeScript style and architecture reviewer. Review the code for
        naming conventions, complexity, and best practices. Return JSON:
        {suggestions: [{type, line, message}], overall_grade: A|B|C|D|F}
      temperature: 0.2
      max_tokens: 1024

    - id: report-synthesizer
      name: Report Synthesizer
      model: claude-sonnet-4-6
      provider: anthropic
      system_prompt: |
        Combine the security scan and style review results into a single,
        readable markdown report suitable for a pull request comment.
      temperature: 0.3
      max_tokens: 2048

  nodes:
    - id: input
      type: input

    - id: fan-out
      type: parallel
      parallel_of: [security-scan-node, style-review-node]

    - id: security-scan-node
      type: agent
      agent_ref: security-scanner
      prompt_template: |
        Review this TypeScript file for security issues:
        ```typescript
        {{ctx.code_content}}
        ```
      timeout_ms: 60000

    - id: style-review-node
      type: agent
      agent_ref: style-reviewer
      prompt_template: |
        Review this TypeScript file for style and architecture:
        ```typescript
        {{ctx.code_content}}
        ```
      timeout_ms: 60000

    - id: merge
      type: merge
      merge_strategy: object_merge

    - id: severity-gate
      type: condition
      condition: 'run.outputs["security-scan-node"].score < 7'
      branches:
        - label: low_score
          condition: 'true'
          target_node: human-approval
        - label: passing
          condition: 'false'
          target_node: synthesize-report

    - id: human-approval
      type: human_gate
      gate_type: approval
      assignee: '{{inputs.reviewer_email}}'
      message_template: |
        Security scan flagged issues in {{inputs.file_path}}.
        Score: {{run.outputs["security-scan-node"].score}}/10.
        Issues found: {{run.outputs["security-scan-node"].issues | length}}.
        Approve to continue to report generation, or reject to halt.
      timeout_ms: 86400000
      timeout_action: reject

    - id: synthesize-report
      type: agent
      agent_ref: report-synthesizer
      prompt_template: |
        Security results: {{run.outputs["security-scan-node"] | json}}
        Style results: {{run.outputs["style-review-node"] | json}}
        Human gate decision: {{run.outputs["human-approval"].decision | default("not required")}}
      timeout_ms: 45000

    - id: output
      type: output
      output_format: markdown

  edges:
    - from: input
      to: fan-out
    - from: fan-out
      to: security-scan-node
    - from: fan-out
      to: style-review-node
    - from: security-scan-node
      to: merge
    - from: style-review-node
      to: merge
    - from: merge
      to: severity-gate
    - from: severity-gate:low_score
      to: human-approval
    - from: severity-gate:passing
      to: synthesize-report
    - from: human-approval
      to: synthesize-report
    - from: synthesize-report
      to: output

topRisks.risk: Tauri WebView rendering inconsistency breaks the ReactFlow canvas on Windows or Linux
topRisks.mitigation: Establish a cross-platform rendering test suite (Playwright + Tauri's test utilities) that runs the canvas on macOS WKWebView, Windows WebView2, and Linux WebKitGTK in CI. Maintain a known-broken CSS list and apply targeted polyfills. If WebView2 on Windows has a blocking regression for a specific ReactFlow feature, the Electron fallback path is pre-evaluated in the frameworkDecision — the monorepo structure ensures the React canvas code is identical; only the Tauri shell would swap to Electron. Pre-validate ReactFlow 11+ and all 9 custom node types on all three platforms before Phase 1 ships.

topRisks.risk: The @flowgent/core engine diverges across surfaces — bugs present on CLI but not desktop, or VS Code extension runs stale engine version
topRisks.mitigation: The engine is a single package (@flowgent/core) with zero platform-specific imports. All surfaces import the exact same package version pinned in their package.json. Turborepo enforces that any surface that depends on @flowgent/core rebuilds when core changes. Integration tests run core directly (not through any surface) in CI. Any surface-specific engine workaround is treated as a bug in core, not a surface patch. The CLI (apps/cli) serves as the canonical integration test harness because it has no UI to hide engine failures.

topRisks.risk: API key leakage — keys exposed in logs, Redis job payloads, exported workflow YAMLs, or browser network tab
topRisks.mitigation: Four independent controls: (1) OS keychain Phase 1 / AES-256-GCM Postgres Phase 2 — keys never written to disk or database in plaintext. (2) @flowgent/core fetches keys from storage at call time; keys are never serialized into BullMQ job payloads, run checkpoints, or log events — enforced by a lint rule that bans the word 'apiKey' in any file under apps/api/src/workers/. (3) Workflow YAML export strips all secret references and replaces them with placeholder tokens — enforced in the WorkflowYAMLSerializer. (4) The browser/portal never calls LLM APIs directly; all LLM calls are proxied through apps/api, so no key ever appears in the browser network tab. A pre-release security audit specifically targets these four surfaces before Phase 2 ships.

immediateNextSteps: Initialize the Turborepo monorepo: pnpm create turbo@latest flowgent --example with-prisma, then restructure to the apps/packages layout defined above

Define the final WorkflowSchema in packages/shared/src/schemas/workflow.ts as a Zod schema — this is the north star that all engine and surface code aligns to; do not proceed to engine coding until this schema is locked

Build the AnthropicAdapter in packages/llm first (it is the primary model), get streaming + tool_call normalization + usage token extraction working with a hardcoded test, then add the Zod-validated response parsing

Stand up the WorkflowEngine.run() method in packages/core against a fixture YAML with a 2-node sequential workflow — get checkpoint write/read/resume working against better-sqlite3 before any surface code is written

Set up Vitest with coverage thresholds (80% line coverage minimum) for packages/shared, packages/llm, and packages/core from day one — the engine is the most critical code in the product and must be regression-proof before surfaces are built on top of it
